introduction.content:
Schema Weaver Documentation¶

Welcome to the official documentation for Schema Weaver. This guide will help you understand the architecture, core concepts, and how to start building amazing schemas and workflows.

What is Schema Weaver?¶

Schema Weaver is a modern, high-performance toolset designed to help you weave complex database schemas, manage data integrity, and visualize connections seamlessly.

Key Features¶

  • Schema Visualization — Beautiful ER diagrams that auto-update as you evolve your schema
  • Smart Migrations — Atlas-powered safe DDL with drift detection and rollback support
  • Data Explorer — Query your database with an AI-assisted interface
  • Real-time Collaboration — Work together on schema changes with built-in version history
  • Compliance Ready — Audit trails, access controls, and migration versioning for regulated industries

Getting Started¶

Ready to dive in? Here's the quickest path:

  1. Install Schema Weaver — npm install schema-weaver or use our cloud platform
  2. Connect Your Database — Point to PostgreSQL, MySQL, or other supported databases
  3. Visualize Your Schema — Auto-generates an interactive ER diagram
  4. Plan Migrations — Use our migration engine to safely evolve your schema
  5. Deploy Changes — Apply migrations with confidence and zero downtime

Quick Navigation¶

Popular Topics¶

  • Getting Started Guide — 5-minute setup walkthrough
  • Core Concepts — Migrations, drift detection, versioning
  • API Reference — Complete endpoint documentation
  • GitHub Repository — Source code & issues

Why Schema Weaver?¶

Problem: Manual Schema Management¶

Most teams manage database schemas through:

  • Manual SQL scripts (error-prone)
  • Unversioned changes (lost history)
  • No rollback mechanism (risky)
  • Scattered documentation (conflicting sources of truth)

Solution: Schema as Code¶

With Schema Weaver, your schema is:

  • Version controlled — Every change tracked in Git
  • Validated — Drift detection catches unexpected changes
  • Reviewable — PRs for schema changes with migration diffs
  • Safe — Smart migration engine prevents breaking changes
  • Observable — Audit logs and deployment history

For Different Roles¶

Database Administrators

  • Monitor schema drift in real-time
  • Approve migrations before deployment
  • Audit all schema changes with timestamps
  • Manage access controls and permissions

Developers

  • Version schema with application code
  • Preview migrations before applying
  • Rollback safely if issues arise
  • Understand schema dependencies

DevOps / Platform Engineers

  • CI/CD integration for automated deployments
  • Multi-environment management (dev → staging → prod)
  • Disaster recovery with snapshots
  • Compliance reporting and audit trails

Community & Support¶

Have questions? Join our community:

  • Discord — Real-time support from the team
  • GitHub Issues — Report bugs or request features
  • Email Support — support@schema-weaver.io
  • Documentation — You're reading it! 📖

Next Step: Getting Started Guide

# Schema Weaver Documentation

Welcome to the official documentation for Schema Weaver. This guide will help you understand the architecture, core concepts, and how to start building amazing schemas and workflows.

## What is Schema Weaver?

Schema Weaver is a modern, high-performance toolset designed to help you weave complex database schemas, manage data integrity, and visualize connections seamlessly.

### Key Features

- **Schema Visualization** — Beautiful ER diagrams that auto-update as you evolve your schema
- **Smart Migrations** — Atlas-powered safe DDL with drift detection and rollback support
- **Data Explorer** — Query your database with an AI-assisted interface
- **Real-time Collaboration** — Work together on schema changes with built-in version history
- **Compliance Ready** — Audit trails, access controls, and migration versioning for regulated industries

## Getting Started

Ready to dive in? Here's the quickest path:

1. **Install Schema Weaver** — `npm install schema-weaver` or use our cloud platform
2. **Connect Your Database** — Point to PostgreSQL, MySQL, or other supported databases
3. **Visualize Your Schema** — Auto-generates an interactive ER diagram
4. **Plan Migrations** — Use our migration engine to safely evolve your schema
5. **Deploy Changes** — Apply migrations with confidence and zero downtime

## Quick Navigation

### Popular Topics

- **[Getting Started Guide](/docs/getting-started)** — 5-minute setup walkthrough
- **[Core Concepts](/docs/core-concepts)** — Migrations, drift detection, versioning
- **[API Reference](/docs/api-reference)** — Complete endpoint documentation
- **[GitHub Repository](https://github.com/Lnxtanx/schema-weaver)** — Source code & issues

## Why Schema Weaver?

### Problem: Manual Schema Management

Most teams manage database schemas through:
- Manual SQL scripts (error-prone)
- Unversioned changes (lost history)
- No rollback mechanism (risky)
- Scattered documentation (conflicting sources of truth)

### Solution: Schema as Code

With Schema Weaver, your schema is:
- **Version controlled** — Every change tracked in Git
- **Validated** — Drift detection catches unexpected changes
- **Reviewable** — PRs for schema changes with migration diffs
- **Safe** — Smart migration engine prevents breaking changes
- **Observable** — Audit logs and deployment history

## For Different Roles

**Database Administrators**
- Monitor schema drift in real-time
- Approve migrations before deployment
- Audit all schema changes with timestamps
- Manage access controls and permissions

**Developers**
- Version schema with application code
- Preview migrations before applying
- Rollback safely if issues arise
- Understand schema dependencies

**DevOps / Platform Engineers**
- CI/CD integration for automated deployments
- Multi-environment management (dev → staging → prod)
- Disaster recovery with snapshots
- Compliance reporting and audit trails

## Community & Support

Have questions? Join our community:

- **Discord** — Real-time support from the team
- **GitHub Issues** — Report bugs or request features
- **Email Support** — [support@schema-weaver.io](mailto:support@schema-weaver.io)
- **Documentation** — You're reading it! 📖

---

**Next Step:** [Getting Started Guide](/docs/getting-started)

getting/started/configuration.content:
Configuration¶

Schema Weaver is configured through a schema-config.json file in your project root, along with optional environment variables. This page is a complete reference for every available option.

Configuration File Location¶

Schema Weaver looks for config in the following order:

  1. Path specified via --config flag
  2. ./schema-config.json in the current working directory
  3. ~/.schema-weaver/config.json (global user config)
# Use a custom config path
schema-weaver migrate apply --config ./config/production.json

Full Configuration Reference¶

{
  "connections": [...],
  "migrationsDir": "./migrations",
  "snapshotsDir": "./snapshots",
  "driftDetection": { ... },
  "migrations": { ... },
  "logging": { ... },
  "resona": { ... }
}

connections¶

An array of database connection objects.

{
  "connections": [
    {
      "name": "development",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "${DATABASE_URL}",
      "ssl": false,
      "poolSize": 5,
      "queryTimeout": 30000
    },
    {
      "name": "staging",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "${STAGING_DATABASE_URL}",
      "ssl": true,
      "sslMode": "require"
    }
  ]
}

Connection Options¶

Option Type Default Description
name string — Unique identifier for this connection (required)
driver string — Database driver: postgres, mysql, sqlite (required)
url string — Connection URL (required; supports env var substitution)
ssl boolean false Enable SSL/TLS for the connection
sslMode string "prefer" SSL mode: disable, require, verify-ca, verify-full
poolSize number 10 Maximum number of connections in the pool
idleTimeout number 30000 Milliseconds before an idle connection is closed
queryTimeout number 60000 Milliseconds before a query times out
schema string "public" Default schema to use (PostgreSQL only)

Connection URL Formats¶

PostgreSQL:

postgresql://username:password@host:port/database
postgres://username:password@host:port/database?sslmode=require

MySQL:

mysql://username:password@host:port/database
mysql://username:password@host:port/database?charset=utf8mb4

SQLite:

file:./relative/path/to/db.sqlite
file:/absolute/path/to/db.sqlite
file::memory:   ← in-memory (testing only)

migrationsDir¶

Directory where migration files are stored. Defaults to ./migrations.

{
  "migrationsDir": "./db/migrations"
}

Migration filenames must match the pattern YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_description.sql or NNN_description.sql. Schema Weaver applies them in lexicographic order.

snapshotsDir¶

Directory for storing schema snapshots. Defaults to ./snapshots.

{
  "snapshotsDir": "./db/snapshots"
}

Schema snapshots are created automatically after each successful migration. They are JSON files that capture the full schema state.

driftDetection¶

Controls automatic drift detection behavior.

{
  "driftDetection": {
    "enabled": true,
    "interval": "6h",
    "connections": ["production"],
    "alertOn": "warning",
    "webhookUrl": "${DRIFT_WEBHOOK_URL}",
    "ignoreObjects": [
      "public.schema_migrations",
      "public.legacy_*"
    ]
  }
}
Option Type Default Description
enabled boolean false Enable background drift monitoring
interval string "6h" How often to check: "1h", "6h", "24h"
connections string[] all Which connections to monitor
alertOn string "error" Alert threshold: "info", "warning", "error"
webhookUrl string — POST drift reports to this URL
ignoreObjects string[] [] Glob patterns for objects to exclude from drift checks

migrations¶

Fine-grained control over migration behavior.

{
  "migrations": {
    "transactional": true,
    "lockTimeout": 30000,
    "autoSnapshot": true,
    "requireReview": false,
    "dangerousOps": "warn",
    "namingFormat": "timestamp"
  }
}
Option Type Default Description
transactional boolean true Wrap each migration in a transaction
lockTimeout number 30000 Milliseconds to wait for a DB lock before failing
autoSnapshot boolean true Create a snapshot after each successful migration
requireReview boolean false Block migrate apply unless migration has a reviewed: true marker
dangerousOps string "warn" How to handle dangerous DDL: "allow", "warn", "block"
namingFormat string "timestamp" Migration filename format: "timestamp" or "sequential"

Dangerous Operations¶

When dangerousOps is set to "block", Schema Weaver will refuse to apply migrations that contain:

  • DROP TABLE without IF EXISTS
  • DROP COLUMN
  • ALTER COLUMN ... TYPE (type narrowing)
  • TRUNCATE

Override for a specific migration by adding a comment:

-- schema-weaver: allow-dangerous
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN legacy_id;

logging¶

Control what Schema Weaver prints to stdout.

{
  "logging": {
    "level": "info",
    "format": "pretty",
    "outputFile": "./logs/schema-weaver.log"
  }
}
Option Type Default Description
level string "info" Log verbosity: "debug", "info", "warn", "error"
format string "pretty" Output format: "pretty" (human) or "json" (machine)
outputFile string — Also write logs to this file path

resona¶

Configuration for the Resona AI integration (Data Explorer + AI Chat).

{
  "resona": {
    "enabled": true,
    "defaultModel": "gpt-4o-mini",
    "maxQueryRows": 500,
    "allowedConnections": ["development", "staging"],
    "readOnly": true
  }
}
Option Type Default Description
enabled boolean true Enable the Resona AI features
defaultModel string "gpt-4o-mini" Default model for AI chat
maxQueryRows number 1000 Maximum rows returned by any query
allowedConnections string[] all Which connections the Data Explorer can access
readOnly boolean true Restrict Data Explorer to SELECT statements only

Environment Variables¶

Schema Weaver reads the following environment variables. These override the corresponding schema-config.json values.

Variable Description
DATABASE_URL Default database connection URL
SW_CONFIG_PATH Path to schema-config.json
SW_MIGRATIONS_DIR Override migrationsDir
SW_LOG_LEVEL Override logging.level
SW_ENV Active environment name (e.g., production)
SW_API_KEY API key for Schema Weaver cloud
RESONA_API_KEY API key for Resona AI features

Using .env Files¶

Schema Weaver automatically loads .env files in the project root via dotenv:

# .env
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://alice:secret@localhost:5432/myapp_dev
SW_LOG_LEVEL=debug

# .env.production
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://prod_user:${DB_PASS}@db.example.com:5432/myapp
SW_LOG_LEVEL=warn

Never commit .env files with real credentials. Add .env to your .gitignore. Commit a .env.example with placeholder values instead.

Multiple Environments¶

Use separate config files per environment and select them with --config:

config/
├── schema-config.dev.json
├── schema-config.staging.json
└── schema-config.production.json

# Apply to staging
schema-weaver migrate apply --config config/schema-config.staging.json

# Check production drift
schema-weaver drift check --config config/schema-config.production.json

Or use the SW_ENV variable with a single config file that references environment-specific connection strings:

{
  "connections": [
    {
      "name": "default",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "${DATABASE_URL}"
    }
  ]
}

Validating Your Config¶

Run the built-in config validator to catch errors before deploying:

schema-weaver config validate

# ✅ schema-config.json is valid
# ✅ connections[0] (development) — reachable
# ⚠ driftDetection.webhookUrl — not set (optional)

Next Step: Core Concepts

# Configuration

Schema Weaver is configured through a `schema-config.json` file in your project root, along with optional environment variables. This page is a complete reference for every available option.

## Configuration File Location

Schema Weaver looks for config in the following order:

1. Path specified via `--config` flag
2. `./schema-config.json` in the current working directory
3. `~/.schema-weaver/config.json` (global user config)

```bash
# Use a custom config path
schema-weaver migrate apply --config ./config/production.json
```

---

## Full Configuration Reference

```json
{
  "connections": [...],
  "migrationsDir": "./migrations",
  "snapshotsDir": "./snapshots",
  "driftDetection": { ... },
  "migrations": { ... },
  "logging": { ... },
  "resona": { ... }
}
```

---

## `connections`

An array of database connection objects.

```json
{
  "connections": [
    {
      "name": "development",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "${DATABASE_URL}",
      "ssl": false,
      "poolSize": 5,
      "queryTimeout": 30000
    },
    {
      "name": "staging",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "${STAGING_DATABASE_URL}",
      "ssl": true,
      "sslMode": "require"
    }
  ]
}
```

### Connection Options

| Option | Type | Default | Description |
|--------|------|---------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | — | Unique identifier for this connection (required) |
| `driver` | `string` | — | Database driver: `postgres`, `mysql`, `sqlite` (required) |
| `url` | `string` | — | Connection URL (required; supports env var substitution) |
| `ssl` | `boolean` | `false` | Enable SSL/TLS for the connection |
| `sslMode` | `string` | `"prefer"` | SSL mode: `disable`, `require`, `verify-ca`, `verify-full` |
| `poolSize` | `number` | `10` | Maximum number of connections in the pool |
| `idleTimeout` | `number` | `30000` | Milliseconds before an idle connection is closed |
| `queryTimeout` | `number` | `60000` | Milliseconds before a query times out |
| `schema` | `string` | `"public"` | Default schema to use (PostgreSQL only) |

### Connection URL Formats

**PostgreSQL:**
```
postgresql://username:password@host:port/database
postgres://username:password@host:port/database?sslmode=require
```

**MySQL:**
```
mysql://username:password@host:port/database
mysql://username:password@host:port/database?charset=utf8mb4
```

**SQLite:**
```
file:./relative/path/to/db.sqlite
file:/absolute/path/to/db.sqlite
file::memory:   ← in-memory (testing only)
```

---

## `migrationsDir`

Directory where migration files are stored. Defaults to `./migrations`.

```json
{
  "migrationsDir": "./db/migrations"
}
```

> Migration filenames must match the pattern `YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_description.sql` or `NNN_description.sql`. Schema Weaver applies them in lexicographic order.

---

## `snapshotsDir`

Directory for storing schema snapshots. Defaults to `./snapshots`.

```json
{
  "snapshotsDir": "./db/snapshots"
}
```

Schema snapshots are created automatically after each successful migration. They are JSON files that capture the full schema state.

---

## `driftDetection`

Controls automatic drift detection behavior.

```json
{
  "driftDetection": {
    "enabled": true,
    "interval": "6h",
    "connections": ["production"],
    "alertOn": "warning",
    "webhookUrl": "${DRIFT_WEBHOOK_URL}",
    "ignoreObjects": [
      "public.schema_migrations",
      "public.legacy_*"
    ]
  }
}
```

| Option | Type | Default | Description |
|--------|------|---------|-------------|
| `enabled` | `boolean` | `false` | Enable background drift monitoring |
| `interval` | `string` | `"6h"` | How often to check: `"1h"`, `"6h"`, `"24h"` |
| `connections` | `string[]` | all | Which connections to monitor |
| `alertOn` | `string` | `"error"` | Alert threshold: `"info"`, `"warning"`, `"error"` |
| `webhookUrl` | `string` | — | POST drift reports to this URL |
| `ignoreObjects` | `string[]` | `[]` | Glob patterns for objects to exclude from drift checks |

---

## `migrations`

Fine-grained control over migration behavior.

```json
{
  "migrations": {
    "transactional": true,
    "lockTimeout": 30000,
    "autoSnapshot": true,
    "requireReview": false,
    "dangerousOps": "warn",
    "namingFormat": "timestamp"
  }
}
```

| Option | Type | Default | Description |
|--------|------|---------|-------------|
| `transactional` | `boolean` | `true` | Wrap each migration in a transaction |
| `lockTimeout` | `number` | `30000` | Milliseconds to wait for a DB lock before failing |
| `autoSnapshot` | `boolean` | `true` | Create a snapshot after each successful migration |
| `requireReview` | `boolean` | `false` | Block `migrate apply` unless migration has a `reviewed: true` marker |
| `dangerousOps` | `string` | `"warn"` | How to handle dangerous DDL: `"allow"`, `"warn"`, `"block"` |
| `namingFormat` | `string` | `"timestamp"` | Migration filename format: `"timestamp"` or `"sequential"` |

### Dangerous Operations

When `dangerousOps` is set to `"block"`, Schema Weaver will refuse to apply migrations that contain:

- `DROP TABLE` without `IF EXISTS`
- `DROP COLUMN`
- `ALTER COLUMN ... TYPE` (type narrowing)
- `TRUNCATE`

Override for a specific migration by adding a comment:

```sql
-- schema-weaver: allow-dangerous
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN legacy_id;
```

---

## `logging`

Control what Schema Weaver prints to stdout.

```json
{
  "logging": {
    "level": "info",
    "format": "pretty",
    "outputFile": "./logs/schema-weaver.log"
  }
}
```

| Option | Type | Default | Description |
|--------|------|---------|-------------|
| `level` | `string` | `"info"` | Log verbosity: `"debug"`, `"info"`, `"warn"`, `"error"` |
| `format` | `string` | `"pretty"` | Output format: `"pretty"` (human) or `"json"` (machine) |
| `outputFile` | `string` | — | Also write logs to this file path |

---

## `resona`

Configuration for the Resona AI integration (Data Explorer + AI Chat).

```json
{
  "resona": {
    "enabled": true,
    "defaultModel": "gpt-4o-mini",
    "maxQueryRows": 500,
    "allowedConnections": ["development", "staging"],
    "readOnly": true
  }
}
```

| Option | Type | Default | Description |
|--------|------|---------|-------------|
| `enabled` | `boolean` | `true` | Enable the Resona AI features |
| `defaultModel` | `string` | `"gpt-4o-mini"` | Default model for AI chat |
| `maxQueryRows` | `number` | `1000` | Maximum rows returned by any query |
| `allowedConnections` | `string[]` | all | Which connections the Data Explorer can access |
| `readOnly` | `boolean` | `true` | Restrict Data Explorer to `SELECT` statements only |

---

## Environment Variables

Schema Weaver reads the following environment variables. These override the corresponding `schema-config.json` values.

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `DATABASE_URL` | Default database connection URL |
| `SW_CONFIG_PATH` | Path to `schema-config.json` |
| `SW_MIGRATIONS_DIR` | Override `migrationsDir` |
| `SW_LOG_LEVEL` | Override `logging.level` |
| `SW_ENV` | Active environment name (e.g., `production`) |
| `SW_API_KEY` | API key for Schema Weaver cloud |
| `RESONA_API_KEY` | API key for Resona AI features |

### Using `.env` Files

Schema Weaver automatically loads `.env` files in the project root via `dotenv`:

```bash
# .env
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://alice:secret@localhost:5432/myapp_dev
SW_LOG_LEVEL=debug
```

```bash
# .env.production
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://prod_user:${DB_PASS}@db.example.com:5432/myapp
SW_LOG_LEVEL=warn
```

> **Never commit `.env` files with real credentials.** Add `.env` to your `.gitignore`. Commit a `.env.example` with placeholder values instead.

---

## Multiple Environments

Use separate config files per environment and select them with `--config`:

```
config/
├── schema-config.dev.json
├── schema-config.staging.json
└── schema-config.production.json
```

```bash
# Apply to staging
schema-weaver migrate apply --config config/schema-config.staging.json

# Check production drift
schema-weaver drift check --config config/schema-config.production.json
```

Or use the `SW_ENV` variable with a single config file that references environment-specific connection strings:

```json
{
  "connections": [
    {
      "name": "default",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "${DATABASE_URL}"
    }
  ]
}
```

---

## Validating Your Config

Run the built-in config validator to catch errors before deploying:

```bash
schema-weaver config validate

# ✅ schema-config.json is valid
# ✅ connections[0] (development) — reachable
# ⚠ driftDetection.webhookUrl — not set (optional)
```

---

**Next Step:** [Core Concepts](/docs/core-concepts)

getting/started.content:
Getting Started¶

Get Schema Weaver up and running in your local environment with this quick start guide.

Installation¶

Option 1: NPM (Recommended)¶

Install Schema Weaver as an npm package:

npm install schema-weaver

Option 2: Docker¶

Run Schema Weaver in a Docker container:

docker pull schema-weaver/cli:latest
docker run -it schema-weaver/cli:latest

Option 3: Cloud Platform¶

Use our hosted platform at schema-weaver.io (no installation needed).

Basic Setup¶

After installing, initialize the setup in your project root:

npx schema-weaver init

This command generates a schema-config.json file where you can define your database connections:

{
  "connections": [
    {
      "name": "production",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/mydb"
    },
    {
      "name": "staging",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "postgresql://user:password@staging.example.com:5432/mydb"
    }
  ],
  "migrationsDir": "./migrations"
}

Connect Your Database¶

PostgreSQL¶

{
  "driver": "postgres",
  "url": "postgresql://username:password@localhost:5432/database_name"
}

MySQL¶

{
  "driver": "mysql",
  "url": "mysql://username:password@localhost:3306/database_name"
}

SQLite¶

{
  "driver": "sqlite",
  "url": "file:./database.db"
}

Your First Migration¶

Create a migration file:

npx schema-weaver migration create add_users_table

This generates a migration file. Edit it:

-- migrations/001_add_users_table.sql

CREATE TABLE users (
  id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  email TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  name TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);

Preview the migration:

npx schema-weaver migrate --preview

Apply the migration:

npx schema-weaver migrate apply

Verify Installation¶

Check that Schema Weaver is working correctly:

npx schema-weaver status

You should see:

  • ✅ Schema Weaver version
  • ✅ Connected databases
  • ✅ Pending migrations
  • ✅ Last applied migration timestamp

Next Steps¶

Congratulations! You've successfully set up Schema Weaver. Now:

  1. Explore Core Concepts — Learn about migrations, drift detection, and schema analysis
  2. View API Reference — Complete API documentation
  3. Read Best Practices — Recommended workflows and patterns

Troubleshooting¶

"Connection refused"¶

Ensure your database is running and the connection URL is correct:

# Test connection
npx schema-weaver validate-connection

"Permission denied"¶

Your database user needs permissions to:

  • Read schema information
  • Execute DDL statements (CREATE, ALTER, DROP)
  • Read migration history table

Still having issues?¶

Open an issue on GitHub or ask in our Discord community.

Ready to learn more? → Core Concepts

# Getting Started

Get Schema Weaver up and running in your local environment with this quick start guide.

## Installation

### Option 1: NPM (Recommended)

Install Schema Weaver as an npm package:

```bash
npm install schema-weaver
```

### Option 2: Docker

Run Schema Weaver in a Docker container:

```bash
docker pull schema-weaver/cli:latest
docker run -it schema-weaver/cli:latest
```

### Option 3: Cloud Platform

Use our hosted platform at [schema-weaver.io](https://schema-weaver.io) (no installation needed).

## Basic Setup

After installing, initialize the setup in your project root:

```bash
npx schema-weaver init
```

This command generates a `schema-config.json` file where you can define your database connections:

```json
{
  "connections": [
    {
      "name": "production",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/mydb"
    },
    {
      "name": "staging",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "postgresql://user:password@staging.example.com:5432/mydb"
    }
  ],
  "migrationsDir": "./migrations"
}
```

## Connect Your Database

### PostgreSQL

```json
{
  "driver": "postgres",
  "url": "postgresql://username:password@localhost:5432/database_name"
}
```

### MySQL

```json
{
  "driver": "mysql",
  "url": "mysql://username:password@localhost:3306/database_name"
}
```

### SQLite

```json
{
  "driver": "sqlite",
  "url": "file:./database.db"
}
```

## Your First Migration

Create a migration file:

```bash
npx schema-weaver migration create add_users_table
```

This generates a migration file. Edit it:

```sql
-- migrations/001_add_users_table.sql

CREATE TABLE users (
  id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  email TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  name TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);
```

Preview the migration:

```bash
npx schema-weaver migrate --preview
```

Apply the migration:

```bash
npx schema-weaver migrate apply
```

## Verify Installation

Check that Schema Weaver is working correctly:

```bash
npx schema-weaver status
```

You should see:
- ✅ Schema Weaver version
- ✅ Connected databases
- ✅ Pending migrations
- ✅ Last applied migration timestamp

## Next Steps

Congratulations! You've successfully set up Schema Weaver. Now:

1. **[Explore Core Concepts](/docs/core-concepts)** — Learn about migrations, drift detection, and schema analysis
2. **[View API Reference](/docs/api-reference)** — Complete API documentation
3. **[Read Best Practices](/docs/best-practices)** — Recommended workflows and patterns

## Troubleshooting

### "Connection refused"

Ensure your database is running and the connection URL is correct:

```bash
# Test connection
npx schema-weaver validate-connection
```

### "Permission denied"

Your database user needs permissions to:
- Read schema information
- Execute DDL statements (CREATE, ALTER, DROP)
- Read migration history table

### Still having issues?

Open an issue on [GitHub](https://github.com/Lnxtanx/schema-weaver/issues) or ask in our [Discord community](https://discord.gg/schema-weaver).

---

**Ready to learn more?** → [Core Concepts](/docs/core-concepts)

# Or as a project dev dependency

Verify the installation:

schema-weaver --version
# schema-weaver v2.1.0

Step 2 — Initialize Your Project¶

Navigate to your project root and run:

cd my-project
schema-weaver init

This creates two files:

my-project/
├── schema-config.json     ← database connection config
└── migrations/            ← migration files live here
    └── .gitkeep

The generated schema-config.json looks like:

{
  "connections": [

  "migrationsDir": "./migrations",

    "enabled": true,
    "interval": "6h"
  }
}

Step 3 — Connect Your Database¶

{
  "connections": [
    {
      "name": "development",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "postgresql://alice:secret@localhost:5432/my_app_dev"
    }
  ],

{ "url": "${DATABASE_URL}" }

Schema Weaver automatically reads from your environment variables.

Step 4 — Create Your First Migration¶

Create a migration file:

schema-weaver migration create create_users_table

This generates migrations/20240324120000_create_users_table.sql. Open it and add your schema:

  name        TEXT,
  role        TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'member',

COMMIT;

-- DOWN: Rollback this migration
BEGIN;

DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_users_role;
DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_users_email;

  + Column: users.created_at   (TIMESTAMPTZ, DEFAULT NOW())
  + Column: users.updated_at   (TIMESTAMPTZ, DEFAULT NOW())
  + Index:  idx_users_email
  + Index:  idx_users_role

Warnings: none
Risk: LOW

Ready to apply? Run: schema-weaver migrate apply
═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

schema-weaver migrate apply

Output:

Applying migrations to: development
═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  ✓ 20240324120000_create_users_table.sql  (312ms)
═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Connection:   development (PostgreSQL 15.2)
Migrations:   1 applied, 0 pending
Drift status: ✅ Clean
Last applied: 20240324_create_users_table (2 seconds ago)
═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

{
  "connections": [
    {
      "name": "local",
      "driver": "sqlite",
      "url": "file:./dev.db"
    }
  ],
  "migrationsDir": "./migrations"
}

No server setup required — Schema Weaver creates the file automatically.

core/concepts/drift-detection.content:
Drift Detection¶

Drift detection helps you identify when your actual database schema differs from your version-controlled schema.

What is Schema Drift?¶

Schema drift occurs when your production database schema doesn't match what's defined in your migrations:

Version Control (Expected)    vs    Production Database (Actual)
├─ users table                      ├─ users table
├─ id (int pk)                      ├─ id (int pk)
├─ email (text unique) ✓            ├─ email (text unique) ✓
├─ created_at (timestamp) ✓         ├─ created_at (timestamp) ✓
└─ (nothing else)                   ├─ legacy_field (text) ❌ unexpected
                                    └─ status (int) ❌ unexpected

Common Causes¶

1. Hotfixes¶

Emergency SQL run directly in production without committing to version control:

# DON'T DO THIS:
psql production_db -c "ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN emergency_flag BOOLEAN;"

2. Manual Changes¶

Team member modifies schema manually via GUI tools like pgAdmin.

3. Incomplete Migrations¶

Migration file created but not applied to all environments:

Staging:    [✓ Applied] users.phone_number
Production: [✗ Missing] users.phone_number ← DRIFT!

4. Failed Rollbacks¶

Rollback script fails partially, leaving schema in inconsistent state.

5. Third-party Tools¶

Extensions or backups modify schema without integration with Schema Weaver.

Detecting Drift¶

Manual Check¶

npx schema-weaver drift check

Output:

Schema Drift Report
═════════════════════════════════════════

Connection: production
Status: ⚠ DRIFT DETECTED

Unexpected objects (in DB, not in migrations):
  • Column: users.legacy_field (TEXT)
  • Index: idx_old_users_status
  • Constraint: check_valid_status

Missing objects (in migrations, not in DB):
  • Column: users.phone_number (TEXT)

═════════════════════════════════════════
Risk Level: HIGH (unexpected columns)
Recommendation: Investigate and reconcile

Automatic Monitoring¶

Enable periodic drift checks:

{
  "driftDetection": {
    "enabled": true,
    "interval": "6h",
    "alertOn": "error"
  }
}

Checks every 6 hours and alerts on issues.

Resolving Drift¶

1. Document Unexpected Changes¶

If changes are intentional, document them:

npx schema-weaver drift accept --object users.legacy_field

This records: "We intentionally have this field that's not in migrations."

2. Create Missing Migrations¶

If migrations are missing from production, apply them:

npx schema-weaver migrate apply

3. Clean Up Production¶

If unexpected columns exist and are truly unwanted:

# Generate migration to remove unexpected objects
npx schema-weaver migration gen --fix-drift

# Review the generated migration
cat migrations/auto_fix_drift_20240323.sql

# Apply it
npx schema-weaver migrate apply

4. Investigate Root Cause¶

For each drift instance:

  1. When did it happen? — Check migration history timestamps
  2. Who made changes? — Check git blame on migrations/
  3. Why wasn't it tracked? — Process improvement

Next: Versioning

core/concepts.content:
Core Concepts¶

Learn the fundamental concepts that power Schema Weaver and enable safe, version-controlled database schema management.

What is a Schema?¶

A database schema is the structure that defines:

  • Tables — Collections of related data organized in rows and columns
  • Columns — Individual fields with specific data types and constraints
  • Constraints — Rules that enforce data integrity (PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, UNIQUE, CHECK)
  • Indexes — Performance optimizations for faster queries
  • Views — Virtual tables created from queries
  • Functions — Reusable SQL code
  • Triggers — Actions that execute automatically on data changes

Schema Evolution¶

As your application grows, your database schema needs to evolve:

Initial Schema  →  Add features  →  Optimize  →  Refactor  →  Archive
(MVP)              (New tables)    (Indexes)   (Rename)    (Partition)

Schema Weaver helps you manage each step safely.

Key Concepts¶

1. Migrations¶

A migration is a version-controlled script that changes your database schema.

Instead of running SQL manually, migrations:

  • Are tracked in version control (Git)
  • Can be previewed before applying
  • Support rollback if issues arise
  • Include execution timestamps and authors

Example Migration:

-- UP: Apply this migration
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN phone_number TEXT;
CREATE INDEX idx_users_phone ON users(phone_number);

-- DOWN: Rollback this migration
DROP INDEX idx_users_phone;
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN phone_number;

2. Schema Drift¶

Schema drift occurs when your actual database schema differs from your source of truth.

Expected Schema (in Git)    vs    Actual Schema (in Database)
├─ users table                     ├─ users table
├─ id column (int)                 ├─ id column (int)
├─ email column (text)             ├─ email column (text)
└─ phone column (text) ✓           └─ phone column (text) MISSING ❌
                                   └─ legacy_id column ❌ (unexpected)

This can happen when:

  • Direct SQL is run against production (bypassing migrations)
  • Team members forget to document schema changes
  • Hotfixes are applied without versioning
  • Integration bugs corrupt the schema

Schema Weaver detects drift automatically and alerts you.

3. Schema Snapshots¶

A snapshot is a point-in-time capture of your entire database schema.

Snapshots enable:

  • Backup and restore
  • Disaster recovery
  • Comparing schemas across environments
  • Understanding schema at a specific commit

Schema Weaver creates snapshots automatically after each migration.

4. Safe Migrations¶

Some migrations are destructive and can cause data loss:

-- DANGEROUS: Drops column and all data
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN email;

-- DANGEROUS: Renames table without data migration
ALTER TABLE users RENAME TO members;

-- DANGEROUS: Changes column type and loses precision
ALTER TABLE products ALTER COLUMN price TYPE INT;

Schema Weaver's Smart Migration Engine detects these and:

  • Prevents accidental destructive changes
  • Suggests safer alternatives (rename vs drop)
  • Requires explicit confirmation (--force)
  • Logs audit trail of who approved dangerous changes

5. Version Control¶

Your schema is version-controlled just like your application code:

git log --oneline migrations/

abc1234 chore: add phone_number column to users
def5678 feat: create products table with indexes
ghi9012 fix: fix migration conflict in dev

This enables:

  • History of who changed what and when
  • Code review before schema changes
  • Rollback to previous schema
  • Understand why changes were made (commit messages)

Next Steps¶

Now that you understand the fundamentals, dive deeper:

  • Migrations — Learn how to create and manage safe migrations
  • Drift Detection — Detect and fix unexpected schema changes
  • Versioning — Manage schema versions and compatibility

- Logs audit trail of who approved dangerous changes

### 5. Version Control

Your schema is version-controlled just like your application code:

```
git log --oneline migrations/

abc1234 chore: add phone_number column to users
def5678 feat: create products table with indexes
ghi9012 fix: fix migration conflict in dev
```

This enables:
- History of who changed what and when
- Code review before schema changes
- Rollback to previous schema
- Understand why changes were made (commit messages)

## Next Steps

Now that you understand the fundamentals, dive deeper:

- **[Migrations](/docs/core-concepts/migrations)** — Learn how to create and manage safe migrations
- **[Drift Detection](/docs/core-concepts/drift-detection)** — Detect and fix unexpected schema changes
- **[Versioning](/docs/core-concepts/versioning)** — Manage schema versions and compatibility

core/concepts/migrations.content:
Migrations¶

Master the Schema Weaver migration system for safe, version-controlled database changes.

What is a Migration?¶

A migration is a timestamped, version-controlled script that modifies your database schema.

1. Write migration (SQL)
   ↓
2. Preview changes
   ↓
3. Code review in Git
   ↓
4. Apply to staging
   ↓
5. Deploy to production

Creating Migrations¶

Automatic Generation (Recommended)¶

Let Schema Weaver generate migrations from your schema definitions:

# Start with your schema file (migrations/schema.hcl)
npx schema-weaver migration gen

This inspects your database and generates the migration:

-- migrations/20240323_add_users_table.sql

CREATE TABLE users (
  id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  email TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

Manual Creation¶

For complex changes, write migrations manually:

npx schema-weaver migration create add_phone_to_users

Generates:

-- migrations/20240323_add_phone_to_users.sql
-- This is an empty migration. Add your SQL below.

-- UP (apply this migration)
BEGIN;
  -- Your SQL here
COMMIT;

-- DOWN (rollback this migration)
BEGIN;
  -- Rollback SQL here
COMMIT;

Migration Workflow¶

1. Preview¶

Before applying, always preview:

npx schema-weaver migrate --preview

Output:

✓ Migration: 20240323_add_phone_to_users.sql

Changes:
  + Column: users.phone_number (TEXT)
  + Index: idx_users_phone

Warnings:
  ⚠ This adds a NOT NULL column without default value
    → Rows will be NULL initially
    → Consider adding DEFAULT or allowing NULL

2. Review¶

Commit and request code review:

git add migrations/
git commit -m "feat: add phone number support to users table"
git push origin feature/phone-numbers

Reviewers check:

  • ✅ SQL is syntactically correct
  • ✅ Column types and constraints make sense
  • ✅ Indexes are placed on frequently queried columns
  • ✅ No destructive changes without justification

3. Test in Staging¶

Apply to staging environment:

npx schema-weaver migrate apply --env staging

Run your test suite against the new schema:

npm test

4. Deploy to Production¶

npx schema-weaver migrate apply --env production

Schema Weaver:

  • Acquires a lock to prevent concurrent changes
  • Checks for schema drift before applying
  • Executes migration in a transaction
  • Records execution timestamp and user
  • Creates schema snapshot after success

Safe Migration Patterns¶

Adding Columns¶

Wrong (will fail for existing rows):

ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN phone_number TEXT NOT NULL;

Correct (allows NULL initially):

ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN phone_number TEXT;
UPDATE users SET phone_number = '(no phone)' WHERE phone_number IS NULL;
ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN phone_number SET NOT NULL;

Renaming Columns¶

Wrong (data loss risk):

ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN email;
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email_address TEXT;

Correct (zero data loss):

ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email_address TEXT;
UPDATE users SET email_address = email;
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN email;

Changing Column Types¶

Wrong (may fail or lose data):

ALTER TABLE products ALTER COLUMN price TYPE INT;

Correct (safe conversion):

ALTER TABLE products ADD COLUMN price_cents INT;
UPDATE products SET price_cents = (price * 100)::INT;
ALTER TABLE products DROP COLUMN price;
ALTER TABLE products RENAME COLUMN price_cents TO price;

Rollback¶

Rollback the last migration:

npx schema-weaver migrate rollback

Rollback to a specific point:

npx schema-weaver migrate rollback --to 20240320_create_users

Migration History¶

View all applied migrations:

npx schema-weaver migrations list

Output:

ID      | Name                         | Applied At           | Duration | Status
--------|------------------------------|----------------------|----------|--------
1       | 001_create_users.sql         | 2024-03-20 10:30 UTC | 245ms    | ✓
2       | 002_add_indexes.sql          | 2024-03-20 10:31 UTC | 156ms    | ✓
3       | 003_add_phone_field.sql      | 2024-03-21 14:22 UTC | 389ms    | ✓

View migration details:

npx schema-weaver migrations show 003_add_phone_field.sql

Best Practices¶

✅ Do¶

  • ✅ Write idempotent migrations (safe to run multiple times)
  • ✅ Keep migrations small (one logical change per migration)
  • ✅ Use transactions to ensure atomicity
  • ✅ Test in staging before production
  • ✅ Include both UP and DOWN scripts
  • ✅ Add comments explaining complex changes
  • ✅ Use meaningful names: add_phone_to_users, not update_schema

❌ Don't¶

  • ❌ Run raw SQL against production
  • ❌ Combine multiple unrelated changes
  • ❌ Skip preview or testing
  • ❌ Ignore rollback scripts
  • ❌ Use auto-migration without review
  • ❌ Assume same SQL works on all databases

Next: Drift Detection

Master the Schema Weaver migration system for safe, version-controlled database changes.

## What is a Migration?

A migration is a timestamped, version-controlled script that modifies your database schema.

```
1. Write migration (SQL)
   ↓
2. Preview changes
   ↓
3. Code review in Git
   ↓
4. Apply to staging
   ↓
5. Deploy to production
```

## Creating Migrations

### Automatic Generation (Recommended)

```bash
# Start with your schema file (migrations/schema.hcl)
npx schema-weaver migration gen
```

```sql
-- migrations/20240323_add_users_table.sql

  id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  email TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

### Manual Creation

```bash
npx schema-weaver migration create add_phone_to_users
```

```sql
-- migrations/20240323_add_phone_to_users.sql
-- This is an empty migration. Add your SQL below.

-- UP (apply this migration)
BEGIN;
  -- Your SQL here
COMMIT;

-- DOWN (rollback this migration)
BEGIN;
  -- Rollback SQL here
COMMIT;
```

## Migration Workflow

### 1. Preview

Before applying, always preview:

```bash
npx schema-weaver migrate --preview
```

Output:
```
✓ Migration: 20240323_add_phone_to_users.sql

Changes:
  + Column: users.phone_number (TEXT)
  + Index: idx_users_phone

Warnings:
  ⚠ This adds a NOT NULL column without default value
    → Rows will be NULL initially
    → Consider adding DEFAULT or allowing NULL
```

### 2. Review

Commit and request code review:

```bash
git add migrations/

git push origin feature/phone-numbers
```

Reviewers check:
- ✅ SQL is syntactically correct
- ✅ Column types and constraints make sense
- ✅ Indexes are placed on frequently queried columns
- ✅ No destructive changes without justification

### 3. Test in Staging

Apply to staging environment:

```bash
npx schema-weaver migrate apply --env staging
```

Run your test suite against the new schema:

```bash
npm test
```

### 4. Deploy to Production

```bash
npx schema-weaver migrate apply --env production
```

Schema Weaver:
- Acquires a lock to prevent concurrent changes
- Checks for schema drift before applying
- Executes migration in a transaction
- Records execution timestamp and user
- Creates schema snapshot after success

## Safe Migration Patterns

### Adding Columns

**Wrong (will fail for existing rows):**
```sql
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN phone_number TEXT NOT NULL;
```

**Correct (allows NULL initially):**
```sql
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN phone_number TEXT;

ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN phone_number SET NOT NULL;
```

### Renaming Columns

**Wrong (data loss risk):**
```sql
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN email;
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email_address TEXT;
```

**Correct (zero data loss):**
```sql
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email_address TEXT;
UPDATE users SET email_address = email;
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN email;
```

### Changing Column Types

**Wrong (may fail or lose data):**
```sql
ALTER TABLE products ALTER COLUMN price TYPE INT;
```

**Correct (safe conversion):**
```sql
ALTER TABLE products ADD COLUMN price_cents INT;
UPDATE products SET price_cents = (price * 100)::INT;
ALTER TABLE products DROP COLUMN price;
ALTER TABLE products RENAME COLUMN price_cents TO price;
```

## Rollback

Rollback the last migration:

```bash
npx schema-weaver migrate rollback
```

Rollback to a specific point:

```bash
npx schema-weaver migrate rollback --to 20240320_create_users
```

## Migration History

View all applied migrations:

```bash
npx schema-weaver migrations list
```

Output:
```
ID      | Name                         | Applied At           | Duration | Status
--------|------------------------------|----------------------|----------|--------
1       | 001_create_users.sql         | 2024-03-20 10:30 UTC | 245ms    | ✓
2       | 002_add_indexes.sql          | 2024-03-20 10:31 UTC | 156ms    | ✓
3       | 003_add_phone_field.sql      | 2024-03-21 14:22 UTC | 389ms    | ✓
```

View migration details:

```bash
npx schema-weaver migrations show 003_add_phone_field.sql
```

## Best Practices

### ✅ Do

- ✅ Write idempotent migrations (safe to run multiple times)
- ✅ Keep migrations small (one logical change per migration)
- ✅ Use transactions to ensure atomicity
- ✅ Test in staging before production
- ✅ Include both UP and DOWN scripts
- ✅ Add comments explaining complex changes
- ✅ Use meaningful names: `add_phone_to_users`, not `update_schema`

core/concepts/versioning.content:
Schema Versioning¶

Learn how to manage schema versions and handle breaking changes gracefully.

Version Your Schema¶

Semantic Versioning¶

Follow Semantic Versioning for your schema:

MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH
  ↓      ↓      ↓
  1      2      3

1.2.3
├─ MAJOR (1): Breaking changes (column dropped, type changed)
├─ MINOR (2): Non-breaking additions (new column, new table)
└─ PATCH (3): Bug fixes (index optimization, constraint fix)

Examples¶

0.1.0 → 1.0.0  (MAJOR: Breaking changes for v1 release)
1.0.0 → 1.1.0  (MINOR: Add new column to users table)
1.1.0 → 1.1.1  (PATCH: Fix missing index on users.email)

Breaking vs Non-Breaking Changes¶

Non-Breaking (MINOR version bump)¶

These are safe and don't require application changes:

✅ Add new column (with default or nullable) ✅ Add new table ✅ Add new index ✅ Create new view ✅ Add new constraint (if existing data satisfies it)

-- Safe: Application doesn't need to change
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN phone_number TEXT;

Breaking (MAJOR version bump)¶

These require application updates:

⚠️ Remove column ⚠️ Remove table ⚠️ Rename column ⚠️ Change column type (narrowing precision) ⚠️ Add NOT NULL constraint without default ⚠️ Drop index (performance impact)

-- Breaking: Application using users.status will break
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN status;

Handling Breaking Changes¶

1. Deprecation Period¶

Before breaking, announce deprecation:

-- v1.2.0: Mark field as deprecated (documentation)
-- Schema change will be in v2.0.0

-- v2.0.0: Now safe to remove
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN legacy_status;

2. Compatibility Layer¶

Support both old and new column names temporarily:

-- v1.1.0: Add new column
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email_address TEXT;

-- Keep old column for compatibility (create view if needed)
CREATE VIEW users_compat AS
SELECT id, email as email_address, created_at FROM users;

Later, after applications migrate:

-- v2.0.0: Remove old column
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN email;

3. Multi-step Migrations¶

For complex changes, split into multiple releases:

v1.2.0: Add new column with default
   ↓ (Wait for application update)
v1.2.1: Copy data from old column to new
   ↓ (Monitor for issues)
v2.0.0: Remove old column

Version Management¶

Tag Versions¶

Tag your schema versions in Git:

git tag schema/v1.2.0 -m "Schema v1.2.0: Add phone field"
git push origin schema/v1.2.0

Document Changes¶

Create a CHANGELOG:

# Schema Changelog

## [1.2.0] - 2024-03-23

### Added
- Column: users.phone_number
- Index: idx_users_phone

### Changed
- users.email now requires unique constraint

## [1.1.0] - 2024-03-15

### Added
- Table: audit_logs
- Column: users.created_at

### Fixed
- Missing index on products.category

Multi-Environment Versioning¶

Track which version is deployed where:

Environment | Version | Last Update       | Status
------------|---------|-------------------|--------
Development | 1.2.1   | 2024-03-23 10:00 | ✓ Latest
Staging     | 1.2.0   | 2024-03-21 15:30 | ⚠ Pending
Production  | 1.1.0   | 2024-03-15 08:45 | ⚠ Outdated

Schema Compatibility Matrix¶

Create a matrix showing which application versions work with which schema versions:

            | Schema 1.0 | Schema 1.1 | Schema 2.0
------------|-----------|-----------|----------
App v1.0    | ✓ OK      | ✓ OK      | ✗ FAIL
App v2.0    | ✗ FAIL    | ✓ OK      | ✓ OK
App v2.1    | ✗ FAIL    | ⚠ Limited | ✓ OK

Use this to plan deployments:

  1. Ensure app and schema versions are compatible
  2. Deploy in correct order (usually schema first, then app)
  3. Verify compatibility after deployment

Done with Core Concepts. Explore the API Reference or return to Introduction.

frontmatter.title: Authentication
frontmatter.description: API key and session token authentication for Schema Weaver REST API

api/reference/authentication.content:
Authentication¶

Schema Weaver's REST API supports two authentication methods: API keys (for machine-to-machine access) and session tokens (for user-facing integrations). All API requests must include one of these.

API Keys¶

API keys are the recommended authentication method for:

  • CI/CD pipelines
  • Backend integrations
  • Scripts and automation
  • Third-party tool connections

Generating an API Key¶

  1. Log in to app.schema-weaver.io
  2. Navigate to Settings → API Keys
  3. Click New API Key
  4. Give it a name (e.g., github-actions-deploy, monitoring-bot)
  5. Select the appropriate permissions scope
  6. Click Generate
  7. Copy the key — it's only shown once

Using an API Key¶

Include the key in the Authorization header as a Bearer token:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer sw_live_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx" \
  https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/connections

// TypeScript example
const response = await fetch('https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/migrations', {
  headers: {
    'Authorization': `Bearer ${process.env.SW_API_KEY}`,
    'Content-Type': 'application/json',
  },
});

# Python example
import requests

headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['SW_API_KEY']}",
}
response = requests.get(
    "https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/connections",
    headers=headers
)

API Key Format¶

Schema Weaver API keys follow this format:

sw_live_[32 random characters]   ← Production key
sw_test_[32 random characters]   ← Test/sandbox key

Test keys work against the same API but are rate-limited and logged separately. Use them for development and testing.

API Key Scopes¶

When creating an API key, assign the minimum permissions needed:

Scope Description
connections:read List and view connections
connections:write Create and update connections
migrations:read View migration history
migrations:write Create and apply migrations
migrations:apply Apply and rollback migrations (subset of write)
schema:read Inspect schema, view drift reports
resona:chat Use the Resona AI chat API
admin Full access (use only for trusted automation)

Principle of least privilege: A CI pipeline that only applies migrations needs migrations:apply and schema:read, not admin.

Scoped Key Example¶

For a CI/CD pipeline:

Scopes: migrations:read, migrations:apply, schema:read

For a monitoring tool:

Scopes: connections:read, schema:read, migrations:read

Session Tokens¶

Session tokens are used for user-context requests. They're issued when a user logs in and expire after 24 hours (or when the user logs out).

Session tokens are typically managed automatically by the Schema Weaver web app and SDKs. You only need to handle them manually for custom integrations.

Obtaining a Session Token¶

curl -X POST https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/auth/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{ "email": "alice@example.com", "password": "supersecret" }'

Response:

{
  "token": "eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...",
  "expires_at": "2024-03-25T10:00:00Z",
  "user": {
    "id": "usr_01hx",
    "email": "alice@example.com",
    "role": "admin"
  }
}

Using a Session Token¶

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..." \
  https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/connections

Refreshing a Session Token¶

Session tokens can be refreshed before they expire:

curl -X POST https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/auth/refresh \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer CURRENT_TOKEN"

Revoking a Session Token¶

curl -X POST https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/auth/logout \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer CURRENT_TOKEN"

OAuth 2.0 (Enterprise)¶

Enterprise plans support OAuth 2.0 for SSO integration with providers like:

  • Okta
  • Azure Active Directory
  • Google Workspace
  • Custom SAML/OIDC providers

Authorization Code Flow¶

1. Redirect user to:
   https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/oauth/authorize
     ?client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID
     &redirect_uri=https://yourapp.com/callback
     &scope=connections:read%20migrations:read
     &state=RANDOM_STATE_VALUE

2. User authenticates with Schema Weaver

3. Schema Weaver redirects to:
   https://yourapp.com/callback?code=AUTH_CODE&state=RANDOM_STATE_VALUE

4. Exchange code for token:
   POST /v1/oauth/token
   {
     "grant_type": "authorization_code",
     "code": "AUTH_CODE",
     "client_id": "YOUR_CLIENT_ID",
     "client_secret": "YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET",
     "redirect_uri": "https://yourapp.com/callback"
   }

5. Response:
   {
     "access_token": "sw_live_xxx",
     "token_type": "Bearer",
     "expires_in": 86400,
     "scope": "connections:read migrations:read"
   }

Contact support@schema-weaver.io to configure OAuth for your enterprise workspace.

Webhook Signatures¶

Schema Weaver sends webhook events (e.g., migration applied, drift detected) signed with HMAC-SHA256. Verify the signature to ensure the webhook is authentic:

import crypto from 'crypto';

function verifyWebhook(
  payload: string,
  signature: string,
  secret: string
): boolean {
  const expected = crypto
    .createHmac('sha256', secret)
    .update(payload)
    .digest('hex');

  return crypto.timingSafeEqual(
    Buffer.from(signature),
    Buffer.from(`sha256=${expected}`)
  );
}

// In your webhook handler:
app.post('/webhooks/schema-weaver', (req, res) => {
  const signature = req.headers['x-schema-weaver-signature'] as string;
  const payload = JSON.stringify(req.body);

  if (!verifyWebhook(payload, signature, process.env.SW_WEBHOOK_SECRET!)) {
    return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid signature' });
  }

  // Process the webhook...
  res.status(200).json({ received: true });
});

Set your webhook secret in the Schema Weaver dashboard under Settings → Webhooks.

Security Best Practices¶

Storing API Keys¶

  • Never hardcode API keys in source code
  • Use environment variables: SW_API_KEY=sw_live_xxx
  • Use secrets managers in production: AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GitHub Secrets
  • Rotate keys periodically (quarterly recommended)

Key Rotation¶

  1. Generate a new API key in the dashboard
  2. Update your environment/secrets manager with the new key
  3. Deploy the updated configuration
  4. Verify the new key is working
  5. Revoke the old key in the dashboard

Avoid deleting a key before confirming the new one works. Keys can be temporarily disabled instead of deleted if you need to pause access.

Monitoring API Key Usage¶

Under Settings → API Keys, each key shows:

  • Last used timestamp
  • Number of requests in the last 30 days
  • IP addresses that have used the key

Set up alerts for unusual activity (spike in requests, new IP addresses) via Settings → Security Alerts.

Authentication Errors¶

Status Error Code Meaning
401 MISSING_AUTH No Authorization header provided
401 INVALID_TOKEN Token is malformed or not recognized
401 EXPIRED_TOKEN Session token has expired (refresh or re-login)
403 INSUFFICIENT_SCOPE API key lacks required permission scope
403 WORKSPACE_SUSPENDED Workspace has been suspended

Example error response:

{
  "error": {
    "code": "INSUFFICIENT_SCOPE",
    "message": "This action requires the 'migrations:apply' scope. Your API key has: connections:read, schema:read.",
    "required_scope": "migrations:apply"
  }
}

Back to: REST API Reference
api/reference/authentication.raw:
---
title: Authentication
description: API key and session token authentication for Schema Weaver REST API
tags: [api, authentication, security, api-key]
order: 3
sidebar_group: "API Reference"
---

# Authentication

Schema Weaver's REST API supports two authentication methods: **API keys** (for machine-to-machine access) and **session tokens** (for user-facing integrations). All API requests must include one of these.

---

## API Keys

API keys are the recommended authentication method for:
- CI/CD pipelines
- Backend integrations
- Scripts and automation
- Third-party tool connections

### Generating an API Key

1. Log in to [app.schema-weaver.io](https://app.schema-weaver.io)
2. Navigate to **Settings → API Keys**
3. Click **New API Key**
4. Give it a name (e.g., `github-actions-deploy`, `monitoring-bot`)
5. Select the appropriate permissions scope
6. Click **Generate**
7. Copy the key — **it's only shown once**

### Using an API Key

Include the key in the `Authorization` header as a Bearer token:

```bash
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer sw_live_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx" \
  https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/connections
```

```typescript
// TypeScript example
const response = await fetch('https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/migrations', {
  headers: {
    'Authorization': `Bearer ${process.env.SW_API_KEY}`,
    'Content-Type': 'application/json',
  },
});
```

```python
# Python example
import requests

headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['SW_API_KEY']}",
}
response = requests.get(
    "https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/connections",
    headers=headers
)
```

### API Key Format

Schema Weaver API keys follow this format:

```
sw_live_[32 random characters]   ← Production key
sw_test_[32 random characters]   ← Test/sandbox key
```

Test keys work against the same API but are rate-limited and logged separately. Use them for development and testing.

---

## API Key Scopes

When creating an API key, assign the minimum permissions needed:

| Scope | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `connections:read` | List and view connections |
| `connections:write` | Create and update connections |
| `migrations:read` | View migration history |
| `migrations:write` | Create and apply migrations |
| `migrations:apply` | Apply and rollback migrations (subset of write) |
| `schema:read` | Inspect schema, view drift reports |
| `resona:chat` | Use the Resona AI chat API |
| `admin` | Full access (use only for trusted automation) |

**Principle of least privilege:** A CI pipeline that only applies migrations needs `migrations:apply` and `schema:read`, not `admin`.

### Scoped Key Example

For a CI/CD pipeline:

```
Scopes: migrations:read, migrations:apply, schema:read
```

For a monitoring tool:

```
Scopes: connections:read, schema:read, migrations:read
```

---

## Session Tokens

Session tokens are used for user-context requests. They're issued when a user logs in and expire after 24 hours (or when the user logs out).

> Session tokens are typically managed automatically by the Schema Weaver web app and SDKs. You only need to handle them manually for custom integrations.

### Obtaining a Session Token

```bash
curl -X POST https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/auth/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{ "email": "alice@example.com", "password": "supersecret" }'
```

**Response:**

```json
{
  "token": "eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...",
  "expires_at": "2024-03-25T10:00:00Z",
  "user": {
    "id": "usr_01hx",
    "email": "alice@example.com",
    "role": "admin"
  }
}
```

### Using a Session Token

```bash
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..." \
  https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/connections
```

### Refreshing a Session Token

Session tokens can be refreshed before they expire:

```bash
curl -X POST https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/auth/refresh \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer CURRENT_TOKEN"
```

### Revoking a Session Token

```bash
curl -X POST https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/auth/logout \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer CURRENT_TOKEN"
```

---

## OAuth 2.0 (Enterprise)

Enterprise plans support OAuth 2.0 for SSO integration with providers like:
- Okta
- Azure Active Directory
- Google Workspace
- Custom SAML/OIDC providers

### Authorization Code Flow

```
1. Redirect user to:
   https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/oauth/authorize
     ?client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID
     &redirect_uri=https://yourapp.com/callback
     &scope=connections:read%20migrations:read
     &state=RANDOM_STATE_VALUE

2. User authenticates with Schema Weaver

3. Schema Weaver redirects to:
   https://yourapp.com/callback?code=AUTH_CODE&state=RANDOM_STATE_VALUE

4. Exchange code for token:
   POST /v1/oauth/token
   {
     "grant_type": "authorization_code",
     "code": "AUTH_CODE",
     "client_id": "YOUR_CLIENT_ID",
     "client_secret": "YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET",
     "redirect_uri": "https://yourapp.com/callback"
   }

5. Response:
   {
     "access_token": "sw_live_xxx",
     "token_type": "Bearer",
     "expires_in": 86400,
     "scope": "connections:read migrations:read"
   }
```

Contact [support@schema-weaver.io](mailto:support@schema-weaver.io) to configure OAuth for your enterprise workspace.

---

## Webhook Signatures

Schema Weaver sends webhook events (e.g., migration applied, drift detected) signed with HMAC-SHA256. Verify the signature to ensure the webhook is authentic:

```typescript
import crypto from 'crypto';

function verifyWebhook(
  payload: string,
  signature: string,
  secret: string
): boolean {
  const expected = crypto
    .createHmac('sha256', secret)
    .update(payload)
    .digest('hex');

  return crypto.timingSafeEqual(
    Buffer.from(signature),
    Buffer.from(`sha256=${expected}`)
  );
}

// In your webhook handler:
app.post('/webhooks/schema-weaver', (req, res) => {
  const signature = req.headers['x-schema-weaver-signature'] as string;
  const payload = JSON.stringify(req.body);

  if (!verifyWebhook(payload, signature, process.env.SW_WEBHOOK_SECRET!)) {
    return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid signature' });
  }

  // Process the webhook...
  res.status(200).json({ received: true });
});
```

Set your webhook secret in the Schema Weaver dashboard under **Settings → Webhooks**.

---

## Security Best Practices

### Storing API Keys

- **Never hardcode** API keys in source code
- Use environment variables: `SW_API_KEY=sw_live_xxx`
- Use secrets managers in production: AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GitHub Secrets
- Rotate keys periodically (quarterly recommended)

### Key Rotation

1. Generate a new API key in the dashboard
2. Update your environment/secrets manager with the new key
3. Deploy the updated configuration
4. Verify the new key is working
5. Revoke the old key in the dashboard

> Avoid deleting a key before confirming the new one works. Keys can be temporarily disabled instead of deleted if you need to pause access.

### Monitoring API Key Usage

Under **Settings → API Keys**, each key shows:
- Last used timestamp
- Number of requests in the last 30 days
- IP addresses that have used the key

Set up alerts for unusual activity (spike in requests, new IP addresses) via **Settings → Security Alerts**.

---

## Authentication Errors

| Status | Error Code | Meaning |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| `401` | `MISSING_AUTH` | No `Authorization` header provided |
| `401` | `INVALID_TOKEN` | Token is malformed or not recognized |
| `401` | `EXPIRED_TOKEN` | Session token has expired (refresh or re-login) |
| `403` | `INSUFFICIENT_SCOPE` | API key lacks required permission scope |
| `403` | `WORKSPACE_SUSPENDED` | Workspace has been suspended |

**Example error response:**

```json
{
  "error": {
    "code": "INSUFFICIENT_SCOPE",
    "message": "This action requires the 'migrations:apply' scope. Your API key has: connections:read, schema:read.",
    "required_scope": "migrations:apply"
  }
}
```

---

**Back to:** [REST API Reference](/docs/api-reference/rest-api)

API Reference¶

Complete documentation of Schema Weaver APIs.

Overview¶

Schema Weaver provides REST APIs for:

  • Migrations — Create, preview, apply, and rollback migrations
  • Schema — Inspect, diff, and validate schemas
  • Connections — Manage database connections
  • Snapshots — Access schema snapshots and history

All APIs require authentication via API key or session token.

Authentication¶

Include your API key in the Authorization header:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" \
  https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/connections

API Endpoints¶

Migrations¶

  • POST /v1/migrations — Create a new migration
  • GET /v1/migrations/:id — Get migration details
  • POST /v1/migrations/:id/preview — Preview migration
  • POST /v1/migrations/:id/apply — Apply migration
  • POST /v1/migrations/:id/rollback — Rollback migration

Schema¶

  • GET /v1/schema — Get current schema
  • POST /v1/schema/diff — Compare two schemas
  • POST /v1/schema/inspect — Inspect schema structure

Connections¶

  • GET /v1/connections — List all connections
  • POST /v1/connections — Create new connection
  • GET /v1/connections/:id — Get connection details
  • PUT /v1/connections/:id — Update connection
  • DELETE /v1/connections/:id — Delete connection

Rate Limiting¶

API requests are rate limited:

  • Free tier: 1,000 requests/day
  • Pro tier: 100,000 requests/day
  • Enterprise: Unlimited

Rate limit headers:

X-RateLimit-Limit: 1000
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 999
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1234567890

# API Reference

Complete documentation of Schema Weaver APIs.

## Overview

Schema Weaver provides REST APIs for:

- **Migrations** — Create, preview, apply, and rollback migrations
- **Schema** — Inspect, diff, and validate schemas
- **Connections** — Manage database connections
- **Snapshots** — Access schema snapshots and history

All APIs require authentication via API key or session token.

## Authentication

Include your API key in the `Authorization` header:

```bash
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" \
  https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/connections
```

## API Endpoints

### Migrations

- `POST /v1/migrations` — Create a new migration
- `GET /v1/migrations/:id` — Get migration details
- `POST /v1/migrations/:id/preview` — Preview migration
- `POST /v1/migrations/:id/apply` — Apply migration
- `POST /v1/migrations/:id/rollback` — Rollback migration

### Schema

- `GET /v1/schema` — Get current schema
- `POST /v1/schema/diff` — Compare two schemas
- `POST /v1/schema/inspect` — Inspect schema structure

### Connections

- `GET /v1/connections` — List all connections
- `POST /v1/connections` — Create new connection
- `GET /v1/connections/:id` — Get connection details
- `PUT /v1/connections/:id` — Update connection
- `DELETE /v1/connections/:id` — Delete connection

## Rate Limiting

API requests are rate limited:

- **Free tier:** 1,000 requests/day
- **Pro tier:** 100,000 requests/day
- **Enterprise:** Unlimited

Rate limit headers:

```
X-RateLimit-Limit: 1000
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 999
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1234567890
```

api/reference/rest-api.content:
REST API Reference¶

All Schema Weaver REST API endpoints are available under:

https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1

For self-hosted deployments, replace the base URL with your instance URL (e.g., http://localhost:4242/api/v1).

Authentication required: All endpoints require an API key or session token. See Authentication.

Connections¶

List Connections¶

GET /v1/connections

Returns all database connections in the workspace.

Response:

{
  "connections": [
    {
      "id": "conn_01hx3gp9d2z4m",
      "name": "production",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "host": "db.example.com",
      "port": 5432,
      "database": "myapp_prod",
      "ssl": true,
      "status": "connected",
      "created_at": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z"
    }
  ]
}

Create Connection¶

POST /v1/connections

Request body:

{
  "name": "staging",
  "driver": "postgres",
  "url": "postgresql://user:pass@staging.example.com:5432/myapp_staging",
  "ssl": true
}

Response: 201 Created with the connection object.

Get Connection¶

GET /v1/connections/:id

Update Connection¶

PUT /v1/connections/:id

Request body: Any fields from the create request.

Delete Connection¶

DELETE /v1/connections/:id

Response: 204 No Content

Test Connection¶

POST /v1/connections/:id/test

Response:

{
  "status": "connected",
  "latency_ms": 12,
  "server_version": "PostgreSQL 15.2",
  "schema_count": 1,
  "table_count": 42
}

Migrations¶

List Migrations¶

GET /v1/migrations?connection_id=conn_01hx3gp9d2z4m&status=applied

Query parameters:

Parameter Type Description
connection_id string Filter by connection
status string applied, pending, failed
limit number Max results (default: 50)
offset number Pagination offset

Response:

{
  "migrations": [
    {
      "id": "mig_01hx3abc",
      "version": "20240324091500",
      "name": "add_verified_to_users",
      "filename": "20240324091500_add_verified_to_users.sql",
      "status": "applied",
      "applied_at": "2024-03-24T09:15:32Z",
      "duration_ms": 245,
      "applied_by": "ci-deploy",
      "checksum": "sha256:abc123..."
    }
  ],
  "total": 12,
  "pending": 0
}

Get Migration¶

GET /v1/migrations/:id

Returns full migration details including the SQL content.

Create Migration¶

POST /v1/migrations

Request body:

{
  "connection_id": "conn_01hx3gp9d2z4m",
  "name": "add_phone_to_users",
  "sql_up": "ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN phone TEXT;",
  "sql_down": "ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN phone;"
}

Response: 201 Created with the migration object.

Preview Migration¶

POST /v1/migrations/:id/preview

Performs a dry-run and returns what changes would be made.

Response:

{
  "migration_id": "mig_01hx3abc",
  "changes": [
    { "type": "add_column", "table": "users", "column": "phone", "data_type": "TEXT" }
  ],
  "warnings": [],
  "risk_level": "low",
  "estimated_duration_ms": 100
}

Apply Migration¶

POST /v1/migrations/:id/apply

Request body:

{
  "connection_id": "conn_01hx3gp9d2z4m",
  "dry_run": false
}

Response:

{
  "migration_id": "mig_01hx3abc",
  "status": "applied",
  "applied_at": "2024-03-24T09:15:32Z",
  "duration_ms": 245,
  "rows_affected": 0
}

Rollback Migration¶

POST /v1/migrations/:id/rollback

Response:

{
  "migration_id": "mig_01hx3abc",
  "status": "rolled_back",
  "rolled_back_at": "2024-03-24T09:20:00Z",
  "duration_ms": 122
}

Schema¶

Get Schema¶

GET /v1/schema?connection_id=conn_01hx3gp9d2z4m

Returns the full schema for a connection.

Query parameters:

Parameter Type Description
connection_id string Required — which connection to inspect
schema string Database schema name (default: public)
include_views boolean Include views (default: true)
include_indexes boolean Include index details (default: true)

Response:

{
  "connection_id": "conn_01hx3gp9d2z4m",
  "schema": "public",
  "inspected_at": "2024-03-24T09:00:00Z",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "users",
      "estimated_rows": 12847,
      "size_bytes": 2097152,
      "columns": [
        {
          "name": "id",
          "type": "bigint",
          "nullable": false,
          "default": "nextval('users_id_seq')",
          "primary_key": true
        }
      ],
      "indexes": [...],
      "foreign_keys": [...]
    }
  ]
}

Diff Schemas¶

POST /v1/schema/diff

Compare the schema of two connections (or a connection against a snapshot).

Request body:

{
  "source": {
    "connection_id": "conn_staging"
  },
  "target": {
    "connection_id": "conn_production"
  }
}

Response:

{
  "diff": {
    "added": [
      { "type": "column", "table": "users", "column": "verified", "data_type": "boolean" }
    ],
    "removed": [],
    "modified": [
      { "type": "index", "table": "orders", "index": "idx_orders_status", "change": "added" }
    ]
  },
  "summary": "1 column added, 1 index added"
}

Inspect Table¶

GET /v1/schema/table?connection_id=conn_01hx&table=users

Returns detailed metadata for a single table.

Drift Detection¶

Check Drift¶

POST /v1/drift/check

Request body:

{
  "connection_id": "conn_01hx3gp9d2z4m"
}

Response:

{
  "connection_id": "conn_01hx3gp9d2z4m",
  "status": "drift_detected",
  "checked_at": "2024-03-24T10:00:00Z",
  "drift": {
    "unexpected": [
      { "type": "column", "table": "users", "name": "legacy_id", "data_type": "integer" }
    ],
    "missing": [
      { "type": "column", "table": "users", "name": "verified", "data_type": "boolean" }
    ]
  },
  "risk_level": "high"
}

Get Drift History¶

GET /v1/drift/history?connection_id=conn_01hx&limit=10

Snapshots¶

List Snapshots¶

GET /v1/snapshots?connection_id=conn_01hx

Get Snapshot¶

GET /v1/snapshots/:id

Create Snapshot¶

POST /v1/snapshots

{
  "connection_id": "conn_01hx3gp9d2z4m",
  "label": "pre-v2-release"
}

Delete Snapshot¶

DELETE /v1/snapshots/:id

Resona AI¶

Chat Completion¶

POST /v1/resona/chat

Request body:

{
  "connection_id": "conn_01hx3gp9d2z4m",
  "messages": [
    { "role": "user", "content": "How many users signed up this week?" }
  ],
  "model": "gpt-4o-mini"
}

Response:

{
  "message": {
    "role": "assistant",
    "content": "There were 347 new users this week.",
    "sql": "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE created_at >= DATE_TRUNC('week', NOW());",
    "result": { "rows": [{ "count": 347 }] }
  },
  "credits_used": 1,
  "credits_remaining": 657
}

Get Usage¶

GET /v1/resona/usage

Returns credit usage for the current billing period.

Error Responses¶

All errors follow this format:

{
  "error": {
    "code": "MIGRATION_CONFLICT",
    "message": "Migration 20240324 conflicts with an already-applied migration.",
    "details": {
      "conflicting_migration": "20240323_similar_change"
    }
  }
}

HTTP Status Codes¶

Code Meaning
200 Success
201 Created
204 No Content (successful delete)
400 Bad Request — invalid parameters
401 Unauthorized — missing or invalid API key
403 Forbidden — insufficient permissions
404 Not Found
409 Conflict — e.g., migration already applied
422 Unprocessable Entity — validation error
429 Too Many Requests — rate limit exceeded
500 Internal Server Error

Next: Authentication

# REST API Reference

```
https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1
```

> **Authentication required:** All endpoints require an API key or session token. See [Authentication](/docs/api-reference/authentication).

---

## Connections


**Response:**

```json
{
  "connections": [
    {
      "id": "conn_01hx3gp9d2z4m",
      "name": "production",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "host": "db.example.com",
      "port": 5432,
      "database": "myapp_prod",
      "ssl": true,

}
```

```
POST /v1/connections
```

```json
{
  "name": "staging",

}
```

**Response:** `201 Created` with the connection object.

### Get Connection

```
GET /v1/connections/:id
```

### Update Connection

## Migrations

### List Migrations

```
GET /v1/migrations?connection_id=conn_01hx3gp9d2z4m&status=applied
```

**Query parameters:**

| Parameter | Type | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|

| `offset` | number | Pagination offset |

**Response:**

```json
{
  "migrations": [
    {
      "id": "mig_01hx3abc",
      "version": "20240324091500",
      "name": "add_verified_to_users",

      "status": "applied",
      "applied_at": "2024-03-24T09:15:32Z",
      "duration_ms": 245,
      "applied_by": "ci-deploy",
```
