resona/ai/ai-chat.content:
AI Chat¶

Resona AI Chat lets you have a back-and-forth conversation with your database. Ask questions in plain English, refine your results, and build up complex analyses step by step — without writing a single line of SQL.

Opening AI Chat¶

From the Data Explorer¶

Click the AI Assist button in the top-right of the query editor, or press Ctrl+Shift+A.

Standalone Chat Panel¶

Click Resona AI in the main navigation sidebar to open the full-width chat interface.

Keyboard Shortcut¶

From anywhere in the app: Ctrl+Shift+R (Mac: ⌘⇧R) opens Resona AI in a floating panel.

Your First Conversation¶

Start simple. You don't need to mention tables or columns — Resona will figure it out from your schema.

You: How many users do we have?

Resona:

Your users table has 12,847 records.

SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_users FROM users;

Result: 12,847

You: How many signed up this month?

Resona:

1,234 users signed up in March 2024.

SELECT COUNT(*) AS new_users
FROM users
WHERE created_at >= DATE_TRUNC('month', NOW());

Notice that Resona remembers the context — you didn't have to say "users" again.

Example Queries¶

Business Metrics¶

"What is our monthly recurring revenue for the last 6 months?"

"Which pricing plan has the highest churn rate?"

"Show me the conversion funnel from signup to first purchase."

"What percentage of users are active (logged in within 30 days)?"

Data Quality¶

"Are there any orders with a null customer_id?"

"Find users with duplicate email addresses."

"Which products have never been ordered?"

"Show me all foreign key violations in the orders table."

Performance Analysis¶

"Which queries have been running for more than 10 seconds? (PostgreSQL)"

"What are the 10 largest tables by size?"

"Show me the slowest endpoints based on the request_logs table."

Schema Understanding¶

"Explain the relationship between users, subscriptions, and invoices."

"What columns in this database store personally identifiable information?"

"How is the products table related to the inventory table?"

Iterative Refinement¶

The best way to use Resona is to start broad and refine. Resona maintains context across the conversation:

"Show me revenue by country last quarter."

SELECT
  billing_country,
  SUM(amount_cents) / 100.0 AS revenue
FROM invoices
WHERE paid_at >= '2024-01-01' AND paid_at < '2024-04-01'
GROUP BY billing_country
ORDER BY revenue DESC;

"Great. Now break that down by month."

SELECT
  billing_country,
  DATE_TRUNC('month', paid_at) AS month,
  SUM(amount_cents) / 100.0 AS revenue
FROM invoices
WHERE paid_at >= '2024-01-01' AND paid_at < '2024-04-01'
GROUP BY billing_country, month
ORDER BY billing_country, month;

"Only show countries with more than $10,000 in revenue."

Resona adds a HAVING clause to the previous query.

Understanding Resona's Responses¶

Each Resona response includes:

  1. Plain English summary — what the answer is
  2. The SQL query — so you can see exactly what ran
  3. Query results — the actual data
  4. Visualization — a chart if the data is suitable
  5. Explanation (optional) — click "Explain this query" for a step-by-step breakdown

Asking for Explanations¶

You can always ask Resona to explain a query it generated:

"Explain what that query is doing."

"Why did you use a LEFT JOIN instead of INNER JOIN?"

"What does the PARTITION BY clause do here?"

Choosing a Model¶

Click the model selector in the chat header to switch models mid-conversation:

Model Speed Accuracy Credits/query
Free model Fast Basic 0
GPT-4o mini Fast Good 1x
GPT-4o Medium Excellent 7x
Claude Sonnet Medium Excellent 14x

Tip: Use GPT-4o mini for quick exploratory questions and save Claude Sonnet or GPT-4o for complex multi-table analysis or when the first attempt doesn't give the right answer.

When Resona Gets It Wrong¶

AI-generated SQL isn't perfect. If Resona's query doesn't look right:

  1. Tell it directly: "That's not quite right — I want orders from this year, not all time."
  2. Be more specific: Mention exact table or column names if needed.
  3. Copy and edit: Click Copy to editor to move the query to the SQL editor and fix it yourself.
  4. Try a different model: Some queries are better handled by Claude Sonnet or GPT-4o.

Reporting a Bad Query¶

If Resona consistently generates wrong SQL for a type of question, use the thumbs-down button on the response to report it. Your feedback improves future responses.

Saving AI-Generated Queries¶

Once Resona generates a useful query, save it to your query library:

  1. Click the Save button on any query in the chat
  2. Give it a name
  3. Access it later from Saved Queries in the Data Explorer

Saved queries preserve the SQL but not the conversation context.

Conversation History¶

Resona maintains up to 50 messages of context in a single conversation. For a fresh start, click New Conversation at the top of the chat panel.

Previous conversations are saved in History (sidebar) for 30 days.

Limitations¶

Limitation Detail
Schema size Works best with schemas under 500 tables; very large schemas may need guidance
Dialect support Best for PostgreSQL; MySQL and SQLite have slightly lower accuracy
Procedural code Cannot write stored procedures or triggers
Cross-database queries Only queries the selected connection
Real-time data Queries run at request time; no streaming

Tips for Better Results¶

  • Name your tables clearly — user_subscriptions is clearer than usrsubs
  • Use the right connection — Make sure the correct database is selected before asking
  • Add date context — "this quarter" is ambiguous; "Q1 2024" is better
  • Mention units — "revenue in dollars" vs "revenue in cents" avoids confusion
  • Start simple — Get a basic query working, then ask Resona to add complexity

Next: Models and Credits

frontmatter.title: Resona AI

resona/ai.content:
Resona AI¶

Resona is Schema Weaver's built-in AI data assistant. It understands your database schema and lets you explore, query, and analyze your data using natural language — no SQL expertise required.

What Resona Can Do¶

Natural Language to SQL¶

Ask questions about your data in plain English. Resona generates optimized SQL, explains it step by step, and runs it for you:

"How many new users signed up each week over the last 3 months?"

SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('week', created_at) AS week,
  COUNT(*) AS new_users
FROM users
WHERE created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '3 months'
GROUP BY week
ORDER BY week;

Automatic Chart Generation¶

When results contain numeric or time-series data, Resona automatically suggests and renders the most appropriate visualization — bar charts, line charts, pie charts, scatter plots.

Schema Explanation¶

Ask Resona to explain your schema:

"What is the relationship between the orders and order_items tables?"

Resona reads the foreign keys, indexes, and column names to give a clear, plain-language explanation.

Query Optimization¶

Paste a slow query and ask Resona to optimize it:

"This query takes 4 seconds. Can you make it faster?"

Resona suggests indexes, rewrites subqueries, and explains the changes.

Data Exploration¶

Start with high-level questions and drill down:

"Which product categories have the highest return rates?" "Show me the top 5 countries by revenue this quarter." "Are there any users who have never placed an order?"

How It Works¶

Resona operates with access to your schema metadata — table names, column names, types, constraints, and foreign key relationships. It does not send your actual data to AI model providers. Only the schema structure and your natural language question are sent.

You type: "Top 10 customers by revenue this month"
     │
     ▼

title: Resona AI
description: Meet Resona, the AI-powered data assistant built into Schema Weaver
tags: [resona, ai, overview, natural-language]
order: 5
sidebar_group: "Resona AI"
---

# Resona AI

Resona is Schema Weaver's built-in AI data assistant. It understands your database schema and lets you explore, query, and analyze your data using natural language — no SQL expertise required.

## What Resona Can Do

### Natural Language to SQL

Ask questions about your data in plain English. Resona generates optimized SQL, explains it step by step, and runs it for you:

> "How many new users signed up each week over the last 3 months?"

```sql
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('week', created_at) AS week,
  COUNT(*) AS new_users
FROM users
WHERE created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '3 months'
GROUP BY week
ORDER BY week;
```

### Automatic Chart Generation

When results contain numeric or time-series data, Resona automatically suggests and renders the most appropriate visualization — bar charts, line charts, pie charts, scatter plots.

### Schema Explanation

Ask Resona to explain your schema:

> "What is the relationship between the `orders` and `order_items` tables?"

Resona reads the foreign keys, indexes, and column names to give a clear, plain-language explanation.

### Query Optimization

Paste a slow query and ask Resona to optimize it:

> "This query takes 4 seconds. Can you make it faster?"

Resona suggests indexes, rewrites subqueries, and explains the changes.

### Data Exploration

Start with high-level questions and drill down:

> "Which product categories have the highest return rates?"
> "Show me the top 5 countries by revenue this quarter."
> "Are there any users who have never placed an order?"

---

## How It Works

Resona operates with access to your schema metadata — table names, column names, types, constraints, and foreign key relationships. **It does not send your actual data to AI model providers.** Only the schema structure and your natural language question are sent.

```
You type: "Top 10 customers by revenue this month"
     │
     ▼
Resona reads your schema (table/column names)
     │
     ▼
Sends to AI model: schema context + your question
     │
     ▼
AI returns: SQL query
     │
     ▼
Resona executes query against YOUR database
     │
     ▼
Results + chart displayed to you
```

Your row-level data never leaves your infrastructure.

---

## Privacy and Security

| What is sent to AI | What stays on your infrastructure |
|--------------------|----------------------------------|
| Table and column names | Actual row data |
| Column types and constraints | Passwords and credentials |
| Foreign key relationships | Query results |
| Your natural language question | Backups and snapshots |

You can optionally **exclude specific tables or columns** from Resona's schema context:

```json
{
  "resona": {
    "schemaExclusions": [
      "public.audit_logs",
      "public.users.password_hash",
      "public.users.ssn"
    ]
  }
}
```

Excluded tables and columns are invisible to Resona entirely.

---

## Models

Resona supports multiple AI models with different capability and cost tradeoffs:

| Model | Credits per query | Best for |
|-------|------------------|----------|
| GPT-4o mini | 1x | Quick lookups, simple queries |
| GPT-4o | 7x | Complex joins, analysis |
| Claude Sonnet | 14x | Nuanced reasoning, long schemas |
| Free model | 0x | Basic queries (rate-limited) |

See [Models and Credits](/docs/resona-ai/models) for full details and plan limits.

---

## Getting Started with Resona

1. Open the [Data Explorer](/docs/data-explorer)
2. Select a database connection from the sidebar
3. Click **AI Assist** or press `Ctrl+Shift+A`
4. Type your question and press Enter

If this is your first time, Resona will index your schema (takes a few seconds for large databases) and then be ready to answer questions.

---

## Plans and Access

| Plan | Resona AI | Monthly Credits | Models Available |
|------|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Free | Limited | 50 credits | Free model only |
| Pro | Full access | 1,000 credits | All models |
| Enterprise | Full access | Custom | All models + fine-tuning |

Credits reset on the 1st of each month. Unused credits do not roll over.

---

- [Data Explorer](/docs/data-explorer) — The workbench that powers Resona

resona/ai/models.content:
Models and Credits¶

Resona AI supports multiple large language models so you can balance speed, quality, and cost. Each query consumes credits from your monthly allowance based on which model you use.

Available Models¶

Model Provider Credits Context Window Best For
Free model Schema Weaver 0 8k tokens Basic lookups, simple queries
GPT-4o mini OpenAI 1x 128k tokens Everyday queries, fast iteration
GPT-4o OpenAI 7x 128k tokens Complex analysis, multi-table joins
Claude Sonnet Anthropic 14x 200k tokens Nuanced reasoning, very large schemas

Free Model¶

The free model is a smaller, fine-tuned model optimized specifically for SQL generation. It's fast and costs zero credits, but:

  • Limited to simpler, single-table queries
  • Less accurate on complex joins and aggregates
  • Rate-limited: 20 queries per hour per user
  • No chart generation

Use the free model when you're exploring a schema or writing straightforward SELECT statements.

GPT-4o mini¶

OpenAI's fastest capable model. Excellent for most Data Explorer use cases:

  • Strong SQL generation across all major dialects
  • Good at multi-table joins and aggregates
  • Chart suggestions included
  • 1 credit per query

This is the recommended default model for everyday use.

GPT-4o¶

OpenAI's most capable model. Use this when GPT-4o mini gives an incorrect or suboptimal query:

  • Best-in-class accuracy for complex analytical queries
  • Handles very large schemas (100+ tables) reliably
  • Explains query optimization in depth
  • 7 credits per query

Claude Sonnet¶

Anthropic's Claude Sonnet model. Particularly strong at:

  • Schemas with complex naming conventions or abbreviations
  • Long, multi-step reasoning chains
  • Explaining schema structure and data relationships clearly
  • Queries requiring deep business logic understanding
  • 14 credits per query

Claude Sonnet has a 200k token context window. This makes it the best choice for extremely large schemas (300+ tables) where fitting the full schema into context matters.

Credit System¶

Credits are the unit of measure for Resona AI usage. Each plan comes with a monthly credit allowance.

How Credits Are Calculated¶

One "credit" is defined as the cost of a single GPT-4o mini query.

Model Relative Cost Example: 100 queries
Free model 0x 0 credits
GPT-4o mini 1x 100 credits
GPT-4o 7x 700 credits
Claude Sonnet 14x 1,400 credits

What Counts as a Query¶

  • Each time you send a message to Resona AI Chat = 1 query
  • Follow-up messages in the same conversation each cost credits
  • Starting a new conversation does not cost credits
  • Viewing query history is free
  • Re-running a saved query does not cost AI credits (the query runs directly, no AI involved)

Credits Do Not Roll Over¶

Monthly credits reset on the 1st of each calendar month. Unused credits are not carried over to the following month.

Plan Credit Limits¶

Plan Monthly Credits Overage
Free 50 credits Blocked until next cycle
Pro 1,000 credits $0.02 per extra credit
Enterprise Custom Negotiated

Free Plan¶

50 credits per month. Practically:

  • 50 queries with GPT-4o mini
  • 7 queries with GPT-4o
  • 3 queries with Claude Sonnet

This is enough for light exploration. For regular use, upgrade to Pro.

Pro Plan¶

1,000 credits per month. Practically:

  • 1,000 queries with GPT-4o mini
  • ~142 queries with GPT-4o
  • ~71 queries with Claude Sonnet
  • Or any mix

Overage charges: $0.02 per credit beyond 1,000. You can set a credit cap to prevent unexpected charges:

Settings → Billing → Resona AI → Monthly Credit Cap: 1,500

When the cap is reached, Resona falls back to the free model automatically (or stops, depending on your setting).

Enterprise Plan¶

Enterprises get a negotiated monthly credit pool shared across all team members. Contact sales to discuss volume pricing and custom model access.

Checking Your Credit Usage¶

In the App¶

Go to Settings → Usage to see:

  • Credits used this month
  • Credits remaining
  • Breakdown by model
  • Per-user usage (admins only)

Via API¶

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" \
  https://api.schema-weaver.io/v1/resona/usage

# Response:
{
  "period": "2024-03-01/2024-03-31",
  "credits_used": 342,
  "credits_limit": 1000,
  "credits_remaining": 658,
  "breakdown": {
    "free_model": 0,
    "gpt-4o-mini": 180,
    "gpt-4o": 112,
    "claude-sonnet": 50
  }
}

Setting Default Model¶

Set the default model for all AI Chat sessions in your config:

{
  "resona": {
    "defaultModel": "gpt-4o-mini"
  }
}

Or per-user via Settings → Resona AI → Default Model.

Model Override per Conversation¶

You can switch models at any point during a conversation using the model selector in the chat header. The switch takes effect on the next message.

Model Selection Guide¶

Use this flowchart to choose the right model:

Is your question simple (single table, basic filter)?
  YES → Free model (if accuracy is okay) or GPT-4o mini
  NO  ↓

Does it involve 3+ tables or complex aggregations?
  YES → GPT-4o
  NO  ↓

Is your schema very large (100+ tables) or oddly named?
  YES → Claude Sonnet
  NO  → GPT-4o mini

Rule of Thumb¶

Start with GPT-4o mini. If the generated SQL is wrong or incomplete, switch to GPT-4o or Claude Sonnet for that question.

Bringing Your Own API Key (BYOK)¶

On the Pro and Enterprise plans, you can use your own OpenAI or Anthropic API keys. This means:

  • Queries are billed to your own account at the provider's rates
  • Credits are NOT consumed for BYOK model queries
  • Your data governance policies apply to the API calls

Configure BYOK in Settings → Resona AI → API Keys:

{
  "resona": {
    "openaiApiKey": "${OPENAI_API_KEY}",
    "anthropicApiKey": "${ANTHROPIC_API_KEY}"
  }
}

With BYOK, Resona still enforces schema exclusions and read-only mode. Only the underlying model API call is routed through your key.

FAQ¶

Q: Does Resona send my data to OpenAI or Anthropic? No. Only your schema metadata (table/column names, types, constraints) and your question text are sent. Query results stay on your infrastructure.

Q: What happens when I run out of credits? You'll see a warning at 80% usage. At 100%, Resona falls back to the free model. You can upgrade your plan or wait until the next monthly reset.

Q: Can I share credits across a team? Yes, on Pro and Enterprise plans, credits are pooled across all workspace members. Admins can set per-user limits under Settings → Team → Credit Limits.

Q: Are there volume discounts? Yes, on Enterprise plans. Contact sales@schema-weaver.io.

Next: Schema Visualization

schema/visualization.content:
Schema Visualization¶

Schema Weaver automatically generates interactive Entity-Relationship (ER) diagrams from your live database. Explore tables, relationships, and constraints visually — no manual diagramming required.

Opening the ER Diagram¶

Cloud Platform¶

Navigate to your project and click Schema in the top navigation. The ER diagram loads automatically from your connected database.

CLI (Local Web UI)¶

schema-weaver ui
# Open http://localhost:4242/schema

The diagram renders in seconds for most schemas. For very large schemas (500+ tables), initial rendering may take 10–15 seconds.

What's Shown in the Diagram¶

Each table is rendered as a card showing:

┌─────────────────────────┐
│  users                  │
├─────────────────────────┤
│ 🔑 id          BIGINT   │
│    email       TEXT     │
│    name        TEXT     │
│    role        TEXT     │
│    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ │
└─────────────────────────┘

Relationship lines connect tables via foreign keys:

users ──────────────< orders
  (one user can have many orders)

orders >──────────── products
  (via order_items join table)

Line styles:

  • Solid line — foreign key constraint enforced at the database level
  • Dashed line — inferred relationship (column name matches but no FK constraint)
  • Arrow style — crow's foot notation: one-to-many, many-to-many, one-to-one

Navigating the Diagram¶

Zoom and Pan¶

Action Mouse Touchpad
Zoom in/out Scroll wheel Pinch
Pan Click and drag background Two-finger drag
Reset view Double-click background —
Fit all tables Click Fit button —

Keyboard Shortcuts¶

Action Shortcut
Fit all to screen F
Zoom to 100% 0
Zoom in +
Zoom out -
Find table Ctrl+F / ⌘F

Finding a Table¶

Press Ctrl+F and type a table name. The diagram scrolls to and highlights the matching table.

Focusing on Relationships¶

Click any table card to highlight it and all its direct relationships. Tables not connected to it are dimmed.

This is useful for understanding the blast radius of a change — "if I change the users table, what other tables are affected?"

Expanding Relationship Depth¶

By default, clicking a table shows depth-1 relationships (directly connected tables). Use the depth selector in the top toolbar to show depth-2 (tables connected to connected tables) or deeper.

Depth 1:  users → orders
Depth 2:  users → orders → order_items → products
Depth 3:  users → orders → order_items → products → categories

Table Details Panel¶

Click a table name to open the details panel on the right side:

Columns Tab¶

Column Shows
Name Column identifier
Type Data type with precision (e.g., VARCHAR(255))
Nullable Whether NULL is allowed
Default Default value or expression
Constraints PK, FK, UNIQUE, CHECK

Indexes Tab¶

Lists all indexes on the table:

idx_users_email       UNIQUE  (email)
idx_users_role        BTREE   (role)
idx_users_created_at  BTREE   (created_at DESC)

Foreign Keys Tab¶

Both inbound and outbound foreign keys:

Outbound (this table references):
  users.id → subscriptions.user_id

Inbound (other tables reference this):
  orders.user_id → users.id
  sessions.user_id → users.id
  audit_log.actor_id → users.id

SQL Tab¶

The full CREATE TABLE statement as it exists in the database.

Layout Options¶

The diagram supports multiple layout algorithms. Switch via View → Layout:

Layout Best For
Auto (force-directed) General use, balances space
Hierarchical Schemas with clear parent-child structure
Circular Highlighting clusters of related tables
Manual Full control — drag and drop tables anywhere

Manual positions are saved per-user and persist across sessions.

Filtering the Diagram¶

For large schemas, filter what's visible:

By Schema (PostgreSQL)¶

Select which PostgreSQL schemas to display (e.g., public, analytics, audit).

By Tag¶

If you've tagged tables in schema-config.json, filter by tag:

{
  "visualization": {
    "tags": {
      "users": ["core", "pii"],
      "orders": ["core", "billing"],
      "audit_log": ["audit"]
    }
  }
}

By Table Name¶

Type in the Filter tables box in the toolbar. Supports wildcards: order* matches orders, order_items, order_history.

Hiding Tables¶

Right-click any table → Hide table. Hidden tables still appear in the sidebar list.

Snapshots and Diff View¶

Viewing a Past Schema¶

Schema Weaver stores schema snapshots after every migration. Navigate to Schema → History to browse past snapshots and view the diagram as it looked at any point in time.

Comparing Two Snapshots¶

Select two snapshots and click Compare. The diff view highlights:

  • Green tables/columns — added
  • Red tables/columns — removed
  • Yellow tables/columns — modified

This is extremely useful for code review: "what exactly changed between last week's deploy and this week's?"

Exporting the Diagram¶

Export the current view via File → Export:

Format Use Case
PNG Screenshots, documentation
SVG Scalable graphics, presentations
PDF Print-ready diagrams
JSON Raw schema data for tooling
SQL (DDL) Reconstructible CREATE TABLE statements

Exports capture only the currently visible tables (respecting filters and hidden tables).

Embedding the Diagram¶

On Pro and Enterprise plans, embed a read-only diagram in your internal docs or wiki using an iframe:

<iframe
  src="https://app.schema-weaver.io/embed/schema/YOUR_PROJECT_ID?token=PUBLIC_EMBED_TOKEN"
  width="100%"
  height="600"
  frameborder="0">
</iframe>

Generate a public embed token under Project Settings → Sharing → Embed Token.

Next Steps¶

  • Migrations Deep Dive — Understand how schema changes are managed
  • Core Concepts: Drift Detection — Keep your diagram in sync with reality
  • API Reference — Access schema data programmatically

| Reset view | Double-click background | — |
| Fit all tables | Click **Fit** button | — |

### Keyboard Shortcuts

| Action | Shortcut |
|--------|---------|
| Fit all to screen | `F` |
| Zoom to 100% | `0` |
| Zoom in | `+` |
| Zoom out | `-` |
| Find table | `Ctrl+F` / `⌘F` |

### Finding a Table

Press `Ctrl+F` and type a table name. The diagram scrolls to and highlights the matching table.

---

## Focusing on Relationships

Click any table card to highlight it and all its direct relationships. Tables not connected to it are dimmed.

This is useful for understanding the blast radius of a change — "if I change the `users` table, what other tables are affected?"

### Expanding Relationship Depth

By default, clicking a table shows depth-1 relationships (directly connected tables). Use the **depth selector** in the top toolbar to show depth-2 (tables connected to connected tables) or deeper.

```
Depth 1:  users → orders
Depth 2:  users → orders → order_items → products
Depth 3:  users → orders → order_items → products → categories
```

---

## Table Details Panel

Click a table name to open the details panel on the right side:

### Columns Tab

| Column | Shows |
|--------|-------|
| Name | Column identifier |
| Type | Data type with precision (e.g., `VARCHAR(255)`) |
| Nullable | Whether NULL is allowed |
| Default | Default value or expression |
| Constraints | PK, FK, UNIQUE, CHECK |

### Indexes Tab

Lists all indexes on the table:

```
idx_users_email       UNIQUE  (email)
idx_users_role        BTREE   (role)
idx_users_created_at  BTREE   (created_at DESC)
```

### Foreign Keys Tab

Both **inbound** and **outbound** foreign keys:

```
Outbound (this table references):
  users.id → subscriptions.user_id

Inbound (other tables reference this):
  orders.user_id → users.id
  sessions.user_id → users.id
  audit_log.actor_id → users.id
```

### SQL Tab

The full `CREATE TABLE` statement as it exists in the database.

---

## Layout Options

The diagram supports multiple layout algorithms. Switch via **View → Layout**:

| Layout | Best For |
|--------|---------|
| Auto (force-directed) | General use, balances space |
| Hierarchical | Schemas with clear parent-child structure |
| Circular | Highlighting clusters of related tables |
| Manual | Full control — drag and drop tables anywhere |

Manual positions are saved per-user and persist across sessions.

---

## Filtering the Diagram

For large schemas, filter what's visible:

### By Schema (PostgreSQL)

Select which PostgreSQL schemas to display (e.g., `public`, `analytics`, `audit`).

### By Tag

If you've tagged tables in `schema-config.json`, filter by tag:

```json
{
  "visualization": {
    "tags": {
      "users": ["core", "pii"],
      "orders": ["core", "billing"],
      "audit_log": ["audit"]
    }
  }
}
```

### By Table Name

Type in the **Filter tables** box in the toolbar. Supports wildcards: `order*` matches `orders`, `order_items`, `order_history`.

### Hiding Tables

Right-click any table → **Hide table**. Hidden tables still appear in the sidebar list.

---

## Snapshots and Diff View

### Viewing a Past Schema

Schema Weaver stores schema snapshots after every migration. Navigate to **Schema → History** to browse past snapshots and view the diagram as it looked at any point in time.

### Comparing Two Snapshots

Select two snapshots and click **Compare**. The diff view highlights:

- **Green** tables/columns — added
- **Red** tables/columns — removed
- **Yellow** tables/columns — modified

This is extremely useful for code review: "what exactly changed between last week's deploy and this week's?"

---

## Exporting the Diagram

Export the current view via **File → Export**:

| Format | Use Case |
|--------|---------|
| PNG | Screenshots, documentation |
| SVG | Scalable graphics, presentations |
| PDF | Print-ready diagrams |
| JSON | Raw schema data for tooling |
| SQL (DDL) | Reconstructible `CREATE TABLE` statements |

Exports capture only the currently visible tables (respecting filters and hidden tables).

---

## Embedding the Diagram

On Pro and Enterprise plans, embed a read-only diagram in your internal docs or wiki using an iframe:

```html
<iframe
  src="https://app.schema-weaver.io/embed/schema/YOUR_PROJECT_ID?token=PUBLIC_EMBED_TOKEN"
  width="100%"
  height="600"
  frameborder="0">
</iframe>
```

Generate a public embed token under **Project Settings → Sharing → Embed Token**.

---

## Next Steps

- [Migrations Deep Dive](/docs/migrations) — Understand how schema changes are managed
- [Core Concepts: Drift Detection](/docs/core-concepts/drift-detection) — Keep your diagram in sync with reality
- [API Reference](/docs/api-reference) — Access schema data programmatically

migrations.content:
Migrations Deep Dive¶

This guide goes beyond the basics to cover production-grade migration workflows: CI/CD integration, zero-downtime patterns, multi-environment management, conflict resolution, and the Atlas engine that powers Schema Weaver's DDL safety checks.

New to migrations? Start with Core Concepts: Migrations for the fundamentals, then come back here.

How Schema Weaver's Migration Engine Works¶

Schema Weaver uses Atlas under the hood for DDL parsing, migration planning, and safety analysis. Atlas understands the semantics of SQL — not just the syntax — and can reason about whether a change is safe.

The Migration Lifecycle¶

1. Author    → Write migration SQL (manual or auto-generated)
2. Lint      → Atlas analyzes for safety issues
3. Preview   → Dry-run against a target DB shows exactly what will change
4. Review    → Team reviews in a pull request
5. Staging   → Apply and test on staging environment
6. Lock      → Schema Weaver acquires advisory lock on the DB
7. Apply     → Migration runs inside a transaction
8. Verify    → Post-migration drift check confirms the result
9. Snapshot  → Schema snapshot saved automatically
10. Unlock   → Advisory lock released

Migration File Format¶

Timestamped Format (Default)¶

migrations/
├── 20240101000000_create_users.sql
├── 20240115143000_add_phone_to_users.sql
└── 20240320090000_create_orders.sql

Naming convention: YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_description.sql

Sequential Format¶

Enable with "namingFormat": "sequential" in config:

migrations/
├── 001_create_users.sql
├── 002_add_phone_to_users.sql
└── 003_create_orders.sql

HCL Format (Atlas Native)¶

Schema Weaver also supports Atlas HCL schema files for declarative schema management:

# schema.hcl
table "users" {
  schema = schema.public
  column "id" {
    type = bigserial
  }
  column "email" {
    type    = text
    null    = false
  }
  column "created_at" {
    type    = timestamptz
    default = sql("NOW()")
  }
  primary_key {
    columns = [column.id]
  }
  index "idx_users_email" {
    columns = [column.email]
    unique  = true
  }
}

Generate a migration from an HCL schema diff:

schema-weaver migration gen --schema schema.hcl

Automatic Migration Generation¶

When you modify your HCL schema file, Schema Weaver computes the diff between the current DB state and the desired schema:

# 1. Edit schema.hcl to add a column
# 2. Generate the migration
schema-weaver migration gen

# Output:
# ✓ Generated: migrations/20240324091500_add_verified_to_users.sql
# Preview:
#   + Column: users.verified (BOOLEAN, DEFAULT false)
#   + Index: idx_users_verified

The generated migration is ready to review and apply.

CI/CD Integration¶

GitHub Actions¶

# .github/workflows/migrate.yml
name: Schema Migrations

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'migrations/**'

jobs:
  migrate-staging:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Schema Weaver
        run: npm install -g schema-weaver

      - name: Lint migrations
        run: schema-weaver migrate lint
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.STAGING_DATABASE_URL }}

      - name: Preview migrations
        run: schema-weaver migrate --preview
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.STAGING_DATABASE_URL }}

      - name: Apply migrations
        run: schema-weaver migrate apply --env staging
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.STAGING_DATABASE_URL }}
          SW_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.SW_API_KEY }}

  migrate-production:
    needs: migrate-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Apply to production
        run: schema-weaver migrate apply --env production
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.PRODUCTION_DATABASE_URL }}
          SW_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.SW_API_KEY }}

GitLab CI¶

# .gitlab-ci.yml
migrate:staging:
  stage: deploy
  image: node:18
  script:
    - npm install -g schema-weaver
    - schema-weaver migrate apply --env staging
  environment:
    name: staging
  only:
    - main
  variables:
    DATABASE_URL: $STAGING_DATABASE_URL

migrate:production:
  stage: deploy
  needs: [migrate:staging]
  script:
    - schema-weaver migrate apply --env production
  environment:
    name: production
  when: manual
  variables:
    DATABASE_URL: $PRODUCTION_DATABASE_URL

Multi-Environment Management¶

Environment Configuration¶

{
  "connections": [
    {
      "name": "development",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "${DEV_DATABASE_URL}"
    },
    {
      "name": "staging",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "${STAGING_DATABASE_URL}"
    },
    {
      "name": "production",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "${PRODUCTION_DATABASE_URL}"
    }
  ]
}

Environment-Specific Migrations¶

For migrations that should only run in specific environments, use conditional comments:

-- schema-weaver: environments=development,staging
-- This migration adds test data and should NOT run in production

INSERT INTO feature_flags (name, enabled) VALUES
  ('new_checkout_flow', true),
  ('beta_dashboard', true);

# This will be skipped in production automatically
schema-weaver migrate apply --env production
# ⚠ Skipping: 20240324_seed_feature_flags.sql (not for production)

Status Across Environments¶

schema-weaver migrate status --all

# Environment   | Applied | Pending | Drift
# development   | 12      | 0       | ✅ Clean
# staging       | 11      | 1       | ✅ Clean
# production    | 10      | 2       | ⚠ Check needed

Zero-Downtime Migration Patterns¶

High-traffic applications cannot afford table locks. Use these patterns to avoid downtime.

Adding a Column (Zero Downtime)¶

-- Step 1: Add nullable column (fast, no lock)
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN display_name TEXT;

-- Step 2: Backfill in batches (run separately, outside migration)
-- (run as a background job, not in the migration file)
UPDATE users
SET display_name = name
WHERE id BETWEEN 1 AND 10000;

-- Step 3: Set NOT NULL after backfill completes
-- (separate migration, deploy after backfill is done)
ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN display_name SET NOT NULL;

Renaming a Column (Expand-Contract Pattern)¶

-- Migration 1: Add new column alongside old one
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email_address TEXT;
UPDATE users SET email_address = email;
-- Deploy application code that writes to BOTH columns

-- Migration 2: (after app is deployed) Drop old column
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN email;
ALTER TABLE users RENAME COLUMN email_address TO email;

Adding a Not-Null Column with a Default¶

-- WRONG (locks table on PostgreSQL < 11):
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending';

-- RIGHT (PostgreSQL 11+ handles this efficiently):
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending';
-- PostgreSQL 11+ uses a fast-path for volatile defaults

-- For older PostgreSQL:
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN status TEXT DEFAULT 'pending';
ALTER TABLE orders ALTER COLUMN status SET NOT NULL;

Creating an Index Without Locking¶

-- WRONG (blocks reads and writes during index build):
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders(status);

-- RIGHT (builds in background, no locks):
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_status ON orders(status);

Schema Weaver's linter warns you when it detects a CREATE INDEX without CONCURRENTLY on a large table.

Rollback Strategies¶

Automatic Rollback on Error¶

If a migration fails mid-execution, the transaction is rolled back automatically (for transactional DDL databases like PostgreSQL):

schema-weaver migrate apply

# ✓ Applying: 20240324_complex_migration.sql
# ✗ Error at line 24: column "nonexistent_col" does not exist
# ↩ Rolling back transaction...
# ✓ Database restored to pre-migration state

Manual Rollback¶

Roll back the most recent migration:

schema-weaver migrate rollback

Roll back to a specific migration:

schema-weaver migrate rollback --to 20240315_create_products

Writing Good DOWN Scripts¶

Every migration should include a DOWN section:

-- UP
BEGIN;
CREATE TABLE subscriptions (
  id         BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id    BIGINT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  plan       TEXT NOT NULL,
  expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ
);
CREATE INDEX idx_subscriptions_user ON subscriptions(user_id);
COMMIT;

-- DOWN
BEGIN;
DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_subscriptions_user;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS subscriptions;
COMMIT;

Not all changes are safely reversible. Dropping a column (and its data) cannot be undone without a backup. Schema Weaver warns you when a DOWN script would cause data loss.

Migration Linting¶

Schema Weaver runs Atlas's linting rules before applying any migration. Run lint manually:

schema-weaver migrate lint

Built-in Lint Rules¶

Rule Description Severity
CD-001 Column drop may cause data loss Error
CD-002 Table drop without IF EXISTS Error
MF-001 Missing DOWN (rollback) script Warning
MF-002 Non-concurrent index creation on large table Warning
CT-001 Column type change may truncate data Error
DS-001 TRUNCATE in migration Error
NX-001 NOT NULL column without DEFAULT or backfill Warning

Override a rule for a specific migration:

-- schema-weaver: ignore CD-001
-- Intentional column drop: legacy_field is unused, confirmed with DBA
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN legacy_field;

Conflict Resolution¶

When two developers create migrations concurrently, you may get conflicts:

migrations/
├── 20240324090000_alice_add_avatar.sql
└── 20240324091000_bob_add_bio.sql

Both try to modify the users table. Schema Weaver detects this:

schema-weaver migrate lint

# ⚠ Conflict: Both 20240324090000 and 20240324091000 modify table "users"
# Recommendation: Merge into a single migration or verify ordering.

Resolve by combining them or verifying that the ordering is intentional:

schema-weaver migrate reorder 20240324090000 20240324091000

Migration History and Audit¶

All applied migrations are recorded in the schema_migrations table in your database:

SELECT * FROM schema_migrations ORDER BY applied_at DESC;

-- id | version                        | applied_at           | duration_ms | applied_by
-- ---|--------------------------------|----------------------|-------------|------------
-- 12 | 20240324091500_add_verified    | 2024-03-24 09:15:32  | 245         | ci-deploy
-- 11 | 20240320150000_create_orders   | 2024-03-20 15:02:11  | 1843        | alice@example.com

View history with the CLI:

schema-weaver migrations list --limit 20

schema-weaver migrations show 20240324091500_add_verified

Next Steps¶

  • Schema Visualization — See your schema changes as an ER diagram
  • Drift Detection — Catch schema changes that bypass migrations
  • API Reference — Trigger migrations programmatically

This guide goes beyond the basics to cover production-grade migration workflows: CI/CD integration, zero-downtime patterns, multi-environment management, conflict resolution, and the Atlas engine that powers Schema Weaver's DDL safety checks.

### The Migration Lifecycle

```
1. Author    → Write migration SQL (manual or auto-generated)
2. Lint      → Atlas analyzes for safety issues
3. Preview   → Dry-run against a target DB shows exactly what will change
4. Review    → Team reviews in a pull request
5. Staging   → Apply and test on staging environment
6. Lock      → Schema Weaver acquires advisory lock on the DB
7. Apply     → Migration runs inside a transaction
8. Verify    → Post-migration drift check confirms the result
9. Snapshot  → Schema snapshot saved automatically
10. Unlock   → Advisory lock released
```

---

## Migration File Format

### Timestamped Format (Default)

```
migrations/
├── 20240101000000_create_users.sql
├── 20240115143000_add_phone_to_users.sql
└── 20240320090000_create_orders.sql
```

### Sequential Format

Enable with `"namingFormat": "sequential"` in config:

```
migrations/
├── 001_create_users.sql
├── 002_add_phone_to_users.sql
└── 003_create_orders.sql
```

### HCL Format (Atlas Native)

Schema Weaver also supports Atlas HCL schema files for declarative schema management:

```hcl
# schema.hcl
table "users" {
  schema = schema.public
  column "id" {
    type = bigserial
  }
  column "email" {
    type    = text
    null    = false
  }
  column "created_at" {
    type    = timestamptz
    default = sql("NOW()")
  }
  primary_key {
    columns = [column.id]
  }
  index "idx_users_email" {
    columns = [column.email]
    unique  = true
  }
```

```bash
schema-weaver migration gen --schema schema.hcl
```

---

## Automatic Migration Generation

When you modify your HCL schema file, Schema Weaver computes the diff between the current DB state and the desired schema:

```bash
# 1. Edit schema.hcl to add a column
# 2. Generate the migration

# Output:
# ✓ Generated: migrations/20240324091500_add_verified_to_users.sql
# Preview:
#   + Column: users.verified (BOOLEAN, DEFAULT false)
#   + Index: idx_users_verified
```

The generated migration is ready to review and apply.

---

## CI/CD Integration

### GitHub Actions

```yaml
# .github/workflows/migrate.yml
name: Schema Migrations

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'migrations/**'

jobs:
  migrate-staging:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Schema Weaver
        run: npm install -g schema-weaver

      - name: Lint migrations
        run: schema-weaver migrate lint

          DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.STAGING_DATABASE_URL }}

      - name: Preview migrations
        run: schema-weaver migrate --preview
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.STAGING_DATABASE_URL }}

      - name: Apply migrations
        run: schema-weaver migrate apply --env staging
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.STAGING_DATABASE_URL }}
          SW_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.SW_API_KEY }}

  migrate-production:
    needs: migrate-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Apply to production
        run: schema-weaver migrate apply --env production
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.PRODUCTION_DATABASE_URL }}
          SW_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.SW_API_KEY }}
```

### GitLab CI

```yaml
# .gitlab-ci.yml
migrate:staging:
  stage: deploy
  image: node:18
  script:
    - npm install -g schema-weaver
    - schema-weaver migrate apply --env staging

    name: staging
  only:
    - main
  variables:
    DATABASE_URL: $STAGING_DATABASE_URL

migrate:production:
  stage: deploy
  needs: [migrate:staging]
  script:
    - schema-weaver migrate apply --env production
  environment:
    name: production
  when: manual
  variables:
    DATABASE_URL: $PRODUCTION_DATABASE_URL
```

---

## Multi-Environment Management

### Environment Configuration

```json
{
  "connections": [
    {
      "name": "development",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "${DEV_DATABASE_URL}"
    },
    {
      "name": "staging",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "${STAGING_DATABASE_URL}"
    },
    {
      "name": "production",
      "driver": "postgres",
      "url": "${PRODUCTION_DATABASE_URL}"
    }
  ]
}
```

### Environment-Specific Migrations

For migrations that should only run in specific environments, use conditional comments:

```sql
-- schema-weaver: environments=development,staging
-- This migration adds test data and should NOT run in production

INSERT INTO feature_flags (name, enabled) VALUES
  ('new_checkout_flow', true),
  ('beta_dashboard', true);
```

```bash
# This will be skipped in production automatically
schema-weaver migrate apply --env production
# ⚠ Skipping: 20240324_seed_feature_flags.sql (not for production)
```

### Status Across Environments

```bash
schema-weaver migrate status --all

# Environment   | Applied | Pending | Drift
# development   | 12      | 0       | ✅ Clean
# staging       | 11      | 1       | ✅ Clean
# production    | 10      | 2       | ⚠ Check needed
```

---

## Zero-Downtime Migration Patterns

High-traffic applications cannot afford table locks. Use these patterns to avoid downtime.

### Adding a Column (Zero Downtime)

```sql
-- Step 1: Add nullable column (fast, no lock)
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN display_name TEXT;

-- Step 2: Backfill in batches (run separately, outside migration)
-- (run as a background job, not in the migration file)
UPDATE users
SET display_name = name
WHERE id BETWEEN 1 AND 10000;

-- Step 3: Set NOT NULL after backfill completes
-- (separate migration, deploy after backfill is done)
ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN display_name SET NOT NULL;
```

### Renaming a Column (Expand-Contract Pattern)

```sql
-- Migration 1: Add new column alongside old one
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email_address TEXT;
UPDATE users SET email_address = email;
-- Deploy application code that writes to BOTH columns

-- Migration 2: (after app is deployed) Drop old column
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN email;
ALTER TABLE users RENAME COLUMN email_address TO email;
```

### Adding a Not-Null Column with a Default

```sql
-- WRONG (locks table on PostgreSQL < 11):
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending';

-- RIGHT (PostgreSQL 11+ handles this efficiently):
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending';
-- PostgreSQL 11+ uses a fast-path for volatile defaults

-- For older PostgreSQL:
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN status TEXT DEFAULT 'pending';
ALTER TABLE orders ALTER COLUMN status SET NOT NULL;
```

### Creating an Index Without Locking

```sql
-- WRONG (blocks reads and writes during index build):
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders(status);

-- RIGHT (builds in background, no locks):
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_status ON orders(status);
```

> Schema Weaver's linter warns you when it detects a `CREATE INDEX` without `CONCURRENTLY` on a large table.

---

## Rollback Strategies

### Automatic Rollback on Error

If a migration fails mid-execution, the transaction is rolled back automatically (for transactional DDL databases like PostgreSQL):

```bash
schema-weaver migrate apply

# ✓ Applying: 20240324_complex_migration.sql
# ✗ Error at line 24: column "nonexistent_col" does not exist
# ↩ Rolling back transaction...
# ✓ Database restored to pre-migration state
```

### Manual Rollback

Roll back the most recent migration:

```bash
schema-weaver migrate rollback
```

Roll back to a specific migration:

```bash
schema-weaver migrate rollback --to 20240315_create_products
```

### Writing Good DOWN Scripts

Every migration should include a DOWN section:

```sql
-- UP
BEGIN;
CREATE TABLE subscriptions (
  id         BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id    BIGINT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  plan       TEXT NOT NULL,
  expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ
);
CREATE INDEX idx_subscriptions_user ON subscriptions(user_id);
COMMIT;

-- DOWN
BEGIN;
DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_subscriptions_user;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS subscriptions;
COMMIT;
```

> Not all changes are safely reversible. Dropping a column (and its data) cannot be undone without a backup. Schema Weaver warns you when a DOWN script would cause data loss.

---

## Migration Linting

Schema Weaver runs Atlas's linting rules before applying any migration. Run lint manually:

```bash
schema-weaver migrate lint
```

### Built-in Lint Rules

| Rule | Description | Severity |
|------|-------------|----------|
| `CD-001` | Column drop may cause data loss | Error |
| `CD-002` | Table drop without IF EXISTS | Error |
| `MF-001` | Missing DOWN (rollback) script | Warning |
| `MF-002` | Non-concurrent index creation on large table | Warning |
| `CT-001` | Column type change may truncate data | Error |
| `DS-001` | TRUNCATE in migration | Error |
| `NX-001` | NOT NULL column without DEFAULT or backfill | Warning |

Override a rule for a specific migration:

```sql
-- schema-weaver: ignore CD-001
-- Intentional column drop: legacy_field is unused, confirmed with DBA
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN legacy_field;
```

---

## Conflict Resolution

When two developers create migrations concurrently, you may get conflicts:

```
migrations/
├── 20240324090000_alice_add_avatar.sql
└── 20240324091000_bob_add_bio.sql
```

Both try to modify the `users` table. Schema Weaver detects this:

```bash
schema-weaver migrate lint

# ⚠ Conflict: Both 20240324090000 and 20240324091000 modify table "users"
# Recommendation: Merge into a single migration or verify ordering.
```

Resolve by combining them or verifying that the ordering is intentional:

```bash
schema-weaver migrate reorder 20240324090000 20240324091000
```

---

## Migration History and Audit

All applied migrations are recorded in the `schema_migrations` table in your database:

```sql
SELECT * FROM schema_migrations ORDER BY applied_at DESC;

-- id | version                        | applied_at           | duration_ms | applied_by
-- ---|--------------------------------|----------------------|-------------|------------
-- 12 | 20240324091500_add_verified    | 2024-03-24 09:15:32  | 245         | ci-deploy
-- 11 | 20240320150000_create_orders   | 2024-03-20 15:02:11  | 1843        | alice@example.com
```

View history with the CLI:

```bash
schema-weaver migrations list --limit 20

schema-weaver migrations show 20240324091500_add_verified
```
